title: Labor debates future, Liberals hold up pamphlet
text:
Welcome back to your weekly federal politics update, where Gina Rushton is filling in for Courtney Gould to get you up to speed on the happenings from Parliament House.

He has been attending the event for four decades but Prime Minister Anthony Albanese entered Labor's national conference looking slightly unsure of himself as the escalator he was on stopped suddenly while he was en route to give his opening address to delegates.

Many Labor MPs and their colleagues might not have noticed, potentially still on a high after experiencing, wait for it, parliament's "best (and only)" rock band, fronted by Tony Burke.

The event cost MPs and their staff significantly less than the $1000 a ticket lobbyists had to fork out to witness the home affairs minister release his inner rockdog.

In 2023, the national conference was all about AUKUS but party officials and rank-and-file members are gathered today in Adelaide for three days of deliberations over an entirely different set of policy positions.

In 2023 the national conference was all about AUKUS but party officials and rank-and-file members are gathered today in Adelaide for three days of deliberations over an entirely different set of policy positions. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

As the ABC's Clare Armstrong revealed this morning, Labor members reached an agreement late last night and will back a national artificial intelligence taskforce that brings workers, unions and experts together to help shape the debate over AI.

Earlier this week, Armstrong revealed the conference would be a battleground over gambling reform with members calling for tougher national regulation. But for all the talk on the "fair" taxation of natural resources, the PM continues to hose down suggestions of a future tax on gas exports. The party will lay down the groundwork for green energy policies and a group of MPs are also calling on the party to sign and ratify the UN treaty on the prohibition of nuclear weapons.

As David Speers wrote this morning, senior Labor figures are eager to avoid a messy debate over Gaza when the issue reaches the conference floor on Saturday. The left and right factions last night agreed on wording in the party's draft platform that calls on Israel to "bring its illegal settlement activity" to an end, while also condemning terrorism and violent extremism "including as perpetrated by Hamas". But with two days to go there remains uncertainty about whether there will be a push for further amendments.

ABC Politics in your inbox Sign up to the ABC Politics newsletter with Courtney Gould

Albanese this morning acknowledged how much cost-of-living pressures are hitting Australians with a side of criticism of politicians exploiting these grievances.

"A list of problems only counts for something if it comes with solutions," he said.

Meanwhile, a pamphlet titled the 2026 ALP National Conference Bingo Card has been circulating around the Adelaide Convention Centre all morning. There are two editions for each faction — tick off a spot on the left card if you overhear someone say they miss Doug Cameron and a spot on the right if you see a man on the floor pacing around to "look important".

Tomorrow night former treasurer Wayne Swan and former Indigenous Australians minister Linda Burney will join a club that includes Julia Gillard and Bob Hawke when they receive life memberships.

Opposition Leader Angus Taylor this week brandished a glossy blue brochure. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

The man with a (previously announced) plan

What if the answer to the Liberal Party's historic polling lows, leadership rumblings and lack of direction is in the palm of Angus Taylor's hands?

The prime minister might have his mint green Medicare card, but this week the opposition leader brandished his own political prop while addressing journalists: a glossy blue brochure.

Just as many people don't need a physical Medicare card in the year 2026, the utility of a pamphlet summarising previously announced Liberal policies is limited beyond a press conference.

Taylor held up the document, "Our Plan", with a pledge to restore Australia's standard of living, fix the economy and protect Australia's way of life.

Just a week after the Liberal party canvassed options to modernise itself with its party faithful to prevent further electoral defeat we have a man with a plan, or at least a pamphlet.

Taylor said the document was an attempt to differentiate the Liberals from the Labor government and "others" — presumably One Nation, who he confirmed his party would not merge with in South Australia.

However the document won't be his defining point of difference from his predecessor Sussan Ley who was no stranger to using a glossy brochure as a prop.

Stella the staffy's prime-time debut

While parliament is on a break Australia's parliamentarians have been using their non-sitting weeks very differently.

This is all to say that while the PM was reportedly enjoying an intimate dinner at a Point Piper mansion with the son of a high-flying Chinese developer on Monday night, Barnaby Joyce was yelling at a dog live on national television.

Loading...

The One Nation MP's interview with Sarah Feguson on ABC's 7.30 was interrupted when he paused to bellow at a Staffy-Border Collie Cross named Stella.

"Your decision to defect to One Nation is dragging you further into a place of intolerance and racial discrimination," Ferguson put to Joyce.

"You're defending your leader, but are you really comfortable with where she is taking you?"

Joyce began to answer before yelling "SIT DOWN" and then apologising: "Sorry, it's a dog."

Stella is only two years old and so is blissfully unaware of how mildly she was reprimanded compared to the pooches of actor Johnny Depp and Amber Heard — in 2015 then agriculture minister Joyce threatened to put down Pistol and Boo after they were brought into the country in contravention of our tight biosecurity laws.

"I verbally assaulted the dog, which I'm incredibly sorry for, and then the dog became more famous," Joyce told Nine's Today Show.

Pauline Hanson, now back in the country, appeared amused by the incident but was less jovial when asked to defend her European travel and hit back at criticism of several inflammatory anti-immigration comments she made while away.

The One Nation leader came under further criticism on Wednesday when she called domestic violence a "scourge" but said it was a "two-way street", while being asked about her message to men who hurt their partners.

Hanson faced swift backlash from government ministers, Liberal MPs and domestic violence advocates.

title: Many of us want to read more. Here's how to do that
text:
How many times have you tried to read but ended up scrolling on your phone instead?

Or haven't been able to find the time to read because of chores and other commitments demanding your attention?

If that sounds familiar, you're not alone.

According to a study by Australia Reads, the nation's peak body focused on getting more of us to read, competing priorities, difficulty concentrating, and a lack of time are the most common barriers.

The study also shows that more than half of us want to read more than we do.

The good news is, even if you are juggling things such as work, uni, raising a family, exercise and staying in touch with loved ones, there are some simple ways to fit reading into your everyday life and make it something you enjoy.

Why reading matters and what else gets in the way of it

Aside from the barriers mentioned above, the Australia Reads study also notes other barriers include:

Difficulty finding the time to read

to read Having low literacy

Struggling to find books to read

to read Not being in an environment that encourages reading

But given the multitude of benefits reading can bring, if you can make it work for you, there are rewards.

The study reveals that reading helps reduce stress, loneliness, and even mortality. It can also increase self-esteem and improve sleep.

Studies have also found it can help us be more empathetic and improve critical thinking skills.

Finding your gateway genre

Author and academic Melanie Saward, a proud Bigambul and Wakka Wakka woman, says certain genres and types of books can serve as an entry point for people who are struggling to get into reading.

Author and academic Melanie Saward says comics can be a great place to start. (Supplied: Jill Kerswill)

Dr Saward, who works as a lecturer at the School of Communication and Arts at the University of Queensland, Meanjin/Brisbane, also conducts research into Indigeneity in fiction.

"[Even] a comic book is a good idea," she says.

"[You can] go from a comic book to a graphic novel to an adventure story with some pictures, and … maybe then become a big fantasy reader and so on."

Another great place to start, she says, is asking a friend or family member who is a reader for book recommendations.

"I think most people will watch something on telly that is recommended to them by a friend. So if you've got a friend who's a reader, tell them what you're looking for."

If you don't know anyone who is a reader, librarians and bookshop owners are more than happy to share book recommendations.

After reading a recommendation from someone, Dr Saward says if you tell them what you loved about it, they can recommend similar books.

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

And if you're not enjoying a book, she says it's OK to abandon it and find something more enjoyable.

"There's no pressure to be a completist. Don't struggle through things."

Fitting reading into busy lives

Many of us have busy lives and struggle to make the time for reading, but by doubling up reading with another task, you may be more likely to fit it in.

"Reading on your commute ... that's where audiobooks are great for me," Dr Saward says.

Bethany Driscoll, Professor in Publishing and Communications at The University of Melbourne/Naarm, agrees.

Bethany Driscoll recommends audiobooks as an alternative. (Supplied)

"Audio works really well for some genres in particular, so maybe more the plot-based ones, where you're excited to find out what happens next, and you can just listen while you're doing other tasks."

She says audio versions are also an easy way to consume books while doing chores or exercising.

How can you make reading a habit?

Dr Saward suggests carving out some time each day to read.

Reading before going to sleep helps her switch off for the day.

" When I fall into bed, if I spend 10 minutes reading, that's 10 minutes I'm not on my phone. It's centred, grounded and relaxed, and it does help me go to sleep. "

Temwani Mazaba is an African-Australian writer, content creator, and founder of Melbourne-based social club The Literary Lounge.

She suggests setting an achievable daily goal.

Temwani Mazaba (left) founded the Melbourne-based social club The Literary Lounge. (Supplied)

"It can be by the page count or by the minute count. So, for me, I start with 20 minutes of reading a day."

Professor Driscoll recommends making reading an intentional mindfulness practice, similar to exercise or spending time in nature.

"Really take that time to slow down and enjoy the sensory experience of reading a book. Maybe you create a little ritual around it," she says.

"You're reading in bed, or you light a candle and you read on the couch, or you read on the beach or in a park."

Ms Mazaba agrees. Don't be surprised if reading feels tiring when you are getting back into it, she says.

"When you first start reading again, especially after a long time, it's going to be tiring. You're going to get sleepy. That's because your body is learning to adjust its entertainment to a whole different system."

Get reading into your algorithm

Social media may be a distraction or barrier to reading, but it's also where you can find strong online communities of readers, such as the BookTok sub-community on TikTok.

These can be great place to find new books and people who are interested in them.

Back in 2024, Ms Mazaba wanted to join a book club but felt like she didn't fit the demographic and were too far away.

So she created her own.

Members discuss their latest read at Temwani's book club in Melbourne. (Supplied: Temwani Mazaba)

"On TikTok, I created a post of me being like, 'Hey, guys, if anyone wants to join a book club …' and surprisingly, the numbers went really, really well.

"That led to me to create my first event about a month later. It was really short timing, but because of the demand, I was like, well, we might as well go full force into it."

According to Professor Driscoll, online book communities also influence publishing trends.

"There's the long-standing trend of copycat books, so if one book does really well with readers, publishers will look to publish something similar in that subgenre … like the ice hockey books."

"Social media obviously does have a problem where the popular books get platformed … but anything that gets people reading, I will never be upset about."

title: Aussie captain relishes 'special' chance 'to take time to be a dad'
text:
Water polo is a brutal sport.

Relentless swimming. Incessant wrestling. Elbows and fists flying around as if the combatants were half-submerged within a flooded UFC cage.

To put it mildly, it is not a place for the faint of heart.

So it is somewhat incongruous, then, to see Australian Sharks captain Nathan Power sitting on the side of the Griffith University pool on the Gold Coast with his son, Sebastian in his arms, feeding him a bottle.

Power, 33, will lead the Australian men's water polo team at the World Aquatics Water Polo World Cup event in Sydney this week, with the Sharks taking on reigning world champions Spain on Thursday.

He is also on six months of parental leave from his role as a manager with KPMG, giving him valuable time to be a dad as well as skippering his nation.

Nathan Power will skipper the team in his home town of Sydney. (Supplied: Water Polo Australia)

"It's been fun to get to do this," Power told ABC Sport, with Sebastian still in his arms.

"To get an opportunity to take time to be a dad, it's really special."

It's created some changes within the team environment too.

"This is the first time in my time with the men's team that there's been a baby on tour — and there's actually two at the moment, Blake Edwards has his son Jude here as well," Power said.

"It makes for a bit of a shift; even at breakfast time now, the boys all roll in and it's a case of, who's gunna take hold of the baby and who wants to look after a baby.

"It's amazing to see how a large group of men have all very quickly clicked into dad mode to help out where they can."

Australian Sharks Water Polo coach Tim Hamill (left) says the addition of kids to camp has been great. (Supplied: Zac Hudson)

Head coach Tim Hamill, who has led the Sharks since taking the reins post-Tokyo in 2021, also made note of the positive changes to the dynamic young children can have.

"It's a first for us," the former international player told ABC Sport.

"One of our values as a team is around family, so we talk to that in having those families here.

The 'precious' bond driving the Stingers to success Australia's women's water polo team, the Stingers, were the most successful Australian team at the Paris Olympics, winning a stunning silver medal. This is the secret to their success.

"We're really fortunate to have a nice balance of the group, we've got the spectrum covered in terms of interests and ideas and for the first time, in this preparation, we've got two of our players who have children, which we've never had before.

"So that changed the dynamic of the group.

"A lot of people have spoken about Paris and how do we get those results [that we got] and I'm conscious to talk culture because it's that overused word in sport at the moment.

"But for me, culture as a word is derived from that care, there's real care in this team.

"We know that our culture needs to be the best in the world, we know that our team care needs to be the best in the world, otherwise we can't compete, so we invest time in all of that, and that's conversations with those 18s all the way through to 30 plus year olds and now fathers."

The topic of keeping mothers in sport has been a hot one in the Australian space for a while now.

In December, the Australian Olympic Committee created the Olympians Future Fund, with a key pillar of that designed to help support female Olympians return to high-performance sport after giving birth, making them eligible to receive $10,000.

Although Power's situation is different, he noted that the support that he has received from KPMG and Water Polo Australia was just as valuable to him in order to allow both he and his partner Maddy to continue their careers.

Water polo is not exactly a gentle sport, as Power shows while wrestling Spain on their way to the world championship title last year. (Getty Images: Adam Pretty)

"That's kind of the thing that can maybe get lost at times, but really it's support for the family," he said.

"With the support that I get through my work at KPMG, that then enables Maddy to get back into work; the support I get from Water Polo Australia, that enables us as a family to be here.

"We both get to keep on pursuing what we want to pursue, but also get to spend some really meaningful time with Sebastian too.

"It's been awesome to come home from training and the first thing I see is a silly smile from the window, him looking out at me.

"It's a new, fun way to come home from training when you're a bit cooked and tired and maybe a bit grumpy."

Power beamed with joy when recounting how much Sebastian was enjoying finding his feet in the water during his lessons, adding that fatherhood has "massively" changed his perspective.

Nathan Power has competing priorities now. (Supplied: Water Polo Australia)

"I think, obviously parenthood becomes that key identity that you have and you see yourself as," he said.

"It's also something which for me, at 33, I guess we can probably confidently say I'm closer to the end than the start of my career, so it means you also get to take some moments and really appreciate that this isn't normal to get to do this and to share this experience with your family.

"It gets you to appreciate these experiences you have with the team too, because they can be very fleeting."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

The World Cup Final is the first time Australia has hosted a major water polo championships since the 2007 World Championships in Melbourne, and the first time Sydney has hosted a combined event since the 2000 Olympics.

Power said the opportunity to compete in front of friends and family will never be undervalued by the squad.

"The importance of it and the rarity of it is not lost on us," Power said.

"I've had teammates who've started their career and gone through their career and finished their career and didn't get that opportunity to be able to play in an event at home like that.

"Obviously, as we move towards Brisbane, this is one of those good examples of something which can hopefully lead to more of these events occurring as well."

title: The former cocaine-dealing Sydney socialite linked to a far-right news site
text:
One News Australia claims to be an independent "right of centre" media outlet, covering "breaking news, global affairs, politics, finance, health and AI".

"We are proudly independent and 100% Australian owned. We are funded by our readers, not by political parties, governments, or undisclosed backers. We have no formal, financial, or organisational ties to any political party, and our editorial choices are entirely our own" its website states.

But an ABC NEWS Verify investigation into Australia's newest media organisation has found it is a pro-One Nation outlet, with a social media page recently rebranded from the "One Nation Supporters Network".

The listed editor of One News Australia does not exist, and the organisation appears to be closely linked to a former Sydney socialite who served jail time for dealing cocaine.

One News Australia and One Nation both say they have no affiliation with one another, but the orange-branded news reports strongly favour One Nation, with some reading more as opinion articles in support of Pauline Hanson.

These news articles are being consumed by the more than 40,000 followers One News Australia has amassed on Facebook alone.

One Nation denies any link between the political party and the "news site". (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

An editor who does not exist

One News Australia registered as a company this year and began posting articles and social media pieces across various platforms in May.

On July 18, it splashed its website and social media pages with the headline "Pauline Hanson has the left green with envy, and her Europe trip didn't cost you single cent".

The 2,400 word article, a defence of Pauline Hanson's recent tour of the United Kingdom, and trip to Sicily with mining billionaire Gina Rinehart, appeared to have been written by the website's editor Brooke Mercer.

But despite having a byline on more than 150 articles, ABC NEWS Verify has not been able to find any evidence Brooke Mercer exists.

The ABC has contacted all publicly listed people who have that name in Australia, who all either denied any association with One News, or did not respond.

One News Australia and One Nation both say they have no affiliation with one another. (Supplied)

After ABC NEWS Verify attempted to contact the owner of One News via its official email address, the website was updated to confirm that the name Brooke Mercer did not refer to a real person.

"Articles are published under the unified masthead byline "Brooke Mercer" to reflect the editorial output of One News Australia," the website now reads.

"Due to direct security concerns and personal threats directed at our team, we utilise this central editorial byline to protect the safety and privacy of our writers."

It is unclear who authors One News's articles but they appear to be a mix of human and AI-generated text.

"We use AI tools in producing this masthead," the website discloses.

"Every article is reviewed by a human editor before publication, and that editor is accountable for what appears," it said.

One News claims to be reader-funded and encourages supporters to contribute via PayPal or cryptocurrency.

One News's website was updated recently to disclose there are no links to One Nation.

"We receive absolutely zero funding, grants, or financial support from One Nation, any other political party, or corporate interest groups," it reads.

"To date, our total community donations stand at less than $500 in aggregate from individual readers.

"We believe in complete transparency about how we operate," the website said.

Company records show the director of One News Australia is James Mathew Winders, seen pictured with Lisa Campanaro. (Supplied)

The 'cocaine socialite'

Company records reveal the director of One News Australia is a 54-year-old man named James Mathew Winders.

He is also the co-director of three other companies alongside 54-year-old Lisa Campanaro and the pair has appeared in photographs together on social media.

Ms Campanaro is also the sole director of a company named Biohax Australia, which trades online as Biohax Wellness.

Biohax Australia and One News Australia are also registered to the same address.

Biohax Wellness's website previously listed James Winders as its founder who "oversees product sourcing, curation and the overall direction of the brand".

The reference to Mr Winders was recently removed.

Biohax Wellness describes itself as a "wellness platform" that is "half online store, half publication" and spruiks products including Dragon's Blood resin and organic dandelion root.

It also publishes wellness news pieces and wellness directories.

Posts on the Biohax Wellness Facebook page and One News featured at least one identical post. (Supplied)

The Facebook pages of Biohax Australia and One News Australia have featured at least one identical post about Anthony Albanese.

According to company records, Lisa Campanaro was previously known as Lisa Stockbridge.

Lisa Danielle Stockbridge was jailed in 2015 after being found guilty of dealing cocaine and MDMA in Sydney.

Police raided her apartment in 2013 and found the drugs, thousands in cash, and 10 mobile phones in a floor safe.

At the time she ran a popular lifestyle blog called Urban Society, which reportedly employed 12 people.

"She turned her entrepreneurial talents to the sale and distribution of cocaine and ecstasy for profit," a judge said in sentencing remarks.

Lisa Campanaro and James Winders did not respond to questions from ABC NEWS Verify.

Do you know more? Email Ruby Jones at ruby.jones.news@proton.me

at If you require more secure communication, please choose an option on the confidential tips page

Recruiting for One Nation

One News Australia's largest social media following is on Facebook, where the page has more than 42,000 followers. The page was originally known as "One Nation Supporters Network" until its name changed in May.

The Facebook page was previously used to direct people towards joining the political party, including by sharing a link to the "join" page on the One Nation official website.

Since it rebranded to One News Australia, the page has continued to post in strong support of One Nation.

There are also several articles defending and spruiking Gina Rinehart.

A spokesperson for Pauline Hanson's One Nation confirmed to ABC NEWS Verify that One News Australia was not affiliated with the party.

Attempts to contact One News Australia went unanswered.

title: Unemployment rate steady in June at 4.4pc
text:
Australia's unemployment rate remained at 4.4 per cent in June, in seasonally adjusted terms.

Data show 76,000 extra people gained employment last month, driven by a 47,000-person rise in part-time employment.

That increase in part-time employment saw the underemployment rate pick up from 6.3 to 6.5 per cent.

Supply shock warning Sarah Hunter says global supply shocks appear to be occurring more frequently and the RBA will try to adjust to the new reality.

In trend terms, the unemployment rate also remained steady at 4.4 per cent.

Economists say the labour market looks resilient in the face of slowing growth, rising inflation, and higher interest rates.

But some say the unemployment rate will increase again in coming months as the longer-term effects of the Iran war play out in Australia's economy.

"We expect the unemployment rate to rise to 4.6 per cent by the end of the year," said Harry McAuley, economist for Oxford Economics Australia.

"However, a renewal of hostilities in the Strait of Hormuz presents a significant risk to the outlook."

What does it means for interest rates?

Economists say it is impressive the labour market is still capable of providing employment for tens of thousands of new people who are entering the labour force to look for work.

They say the participation rate has risen in the last seven months, from 66.6 per cent in November to 67 per cent in June, which highlights how there has been a shift higher in the labour supply.

Australia's job system failing workers A new report argues Australia's employment services system is failing jobseekers.

But they say the underemployment rate has also increased at the same time, from 5.7 per cent in December to 6.5 per cent in June, which shows there has been a noticeable increase in the number of people who would like more hours.

David Bassanese, BetaShares chief economist, said a couple of things could be pushing the participation rate higher.

"It could be that the supply upturn is demand-led, with more Australians being enticed into work by still widely available job opportunities," he said.

" But it could also reflect cost-of-living pressures forcing more Australians into the workforce. "

Overall, he said the "still bountiful employment growth," even if it is possibly supply driven, will still keep the Reserve Bank on edge about possible wage pressures and firmer consumer spending.

"Next week's June quarter consumer price index will 'make or break' the case for an August interest rate increase," he said.

Diana Mousina, AMP deputy chief economist, said the RBA would probably view the unemployment data as indicating that the labour market was still a bit "tight".

"The Reserve Bank meet later in August and we expect another 0.25 per cent rate hike, which would take the cash rate to 4.6 per cent," she said.

"This view is mostly premised on an expectation that next week's June quarter inflation figures will show trimmed mean inflation that is just way too high — running at 3.8 per cent over the year when the RBA is targeting 2.5 per cent."

title: Family takes racing club to court over 'enormous' hidden rubbish dump
text:
Dumped veterinary syringes, horse shoes and rubbish are at the centre of a lawsuit between a family and a regional South Australian horse racing club.

Couple Jacob and Kylie Malthouse have launched legal action against the Mount Gambier Racing Club in the Environment, Resources and Development (ERD) Court, after they discovered a large rubbish pit concealed under land formerly owned by the club.

The Malthouses purchased the land, overlooking the Mount Gambier Racecourse at Glenburnie, in 2024 to build a new home as part of a larger subdivision.

But in January 2025, earthworks uncovered a large rubbish pit underneath where they planned to build the house.

Jacob and Kylie Malthouse reached an agreement to store the waste on the racecourse's land, their claim stated. (ABC South East SA: Josh Brine)

The Malthouse's ERD Court claim alleged the racing club dug a pit on the land some time between 2003 and 2013, which it used to supply sand or loam to the racecourse.

The claim alleged the pit was then used as a dumping ground by the racing club.

It alleged the contents of the pit included veterinary syringes, horseshoes, domestic waste, tree stumps and concrete.

The claim also alleged the pit was then covered and hidden some time around 2013.

"On the first blow, they [the home builders] found rubbish and we thought, 'Oh that's okay, we'll just see what happens,'" Mr Malthouse said.

The rubbish after it was removed from the Malthouses' property. (Supplied: Kylie Malthouse)

"It was enormous. It was 4.9 metres deep and you couldn't even see the excavator in the ground.

"It looked like a tip site; it had temporary fencing, we had the neighbours complaining, we had the poor farmers over the back."

'Created a risk'

The Malthouse family is seeking about $114,000 in compensation from the racing club related to the cost of removing and disposing of the waste and remediating the soil, as well as an amount to compensate the family for time spent cleaning the rubbish.

Between January and March 2025, some of the waste was removed to facilitate the Malthouse's home build and is currently stockpiled on land owned by the racing club, with its consent.

The Malthouses' home is part of a wider subdivision next to the racecourse. (ABC South East SA: Josh Brine)

Alternatively, the claim called for the club to pay the family more than $66,000 and pay to remove the waste currently stockpiled.

The court documents alleged the club contravened the Environment Protection Act by using the pit as a dump site, saying it caused environmental harm.

It also alleged the site was operated as a landfill depot without a licence.

Club rejects claim

The claim alleged the racing club had offered to pay $10,000 to the family for remediation works in February last year.

But in an email the next day, the club retracted the offer and advised it did not accept liability for the waste or the cost of land remediation.

The club is yet to file a defence to the claim with the ERD Court.

A spokesperson for the club said it would respond to the Malthouse's claim "in due course".

The Mount Gambier Racing Club was formed 158 years ago and holds regular meets at its Glenburnie racecourse. (ABC South East SA: Josh Brine)

"Suffice to say the MGRC denies the allegations made by Mr and Mrs Malthouse or that it has any liability to them," they said.

"Further, it denies any breach of any obligation under the Environment Protection Act."

Mrs Malthouse said her family wanted the matter resolved.

"This was supposed to be an enjoyable time for us to build our dream home for our family," she said.

"Instead, we spent weekends with garbage bags battling the wind trying to pick up loose bits of flying rubbish.

"It's just total confusion and shock that we're constantly battling this."

title: Nina Kennedy knows now is the time to soak it all in — if she remembers to
text:
For most of us, falling for 5 metres is terrifying. For Nina Kennedy, provided there's no bar following her down, the fall is the fun part.

But last year introduced her to a fresh fear: the fear that it might all be over.

The reigning Olympic and world champion in pole vault tore her hamstring early in April last year.

Despite the long recovery from surgery, Kennedy was initially named in the Australian squad for the World Athletics Championships in September, but a strained muscle just a couple of days later dragged her back to reality.

How we got to Glasgow 2026 The bumpy road to Glasgow 2026 has produced a Commonwealth Games that will look a little different than the ones that came before.

She had to watch as American rival Katie Moon won outright the title they shared two years earlier.

Yesterday, she was named Australia's flag-bearer for the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony, which felt like a long shot when she was in the depths of rehab less than a year ago.

"Whenever you have a major surgery and you're out for the whole season, there's definitely that doubt of, 'Will this get fully better and will I return to my best and better?'" she said after flying out of a pre-Games athletics training camp early to accept her flag-waving honour.

"You have those thoughts, but you put in the work you do the rehab, you listen to your team, speak to your sport psychologist and never stop dreaming.

"I'm so proud of myself. Of course there were doubts, but I'm lucky enough to do this as a job and I'm pretty damn good at it. I work my arse off every day. So I'm not surprised but I am really proud of myself.

Kennedy has warmed back into the 2026 season after a torn hamstring last year. (Getty Images: Marco Mantovani)

"As athletes we try and get the best out of ourselves, so my mind always goes to, 'What if I don't get to defend my Commonwealth title? What if I don't make it back to the Olympics? What if this hamstring goes again and my career's over and I haven't really soaked up the last few years like I should have?'"

Kennedy has been representing Australia on the world stage for more than a decade, but the past five years since turning to the sport full time have seen her conquer the world and set the Australian women's pole vault record 10 times, including just two weeks ago in Monaco.

The 29-year-old admitted the Brisbane 2032 Olympics would probably be a bridge too far for her, so it was time to look around and enjoy what was happening while she was still on the field.

"Sport can be so fleeting so I've really learned to enjoy every step and this is such a special honour. I'm really going to try and soak this in and make some good memories," she said.

Unfortunately, she knows herself too well and is pretty sure she will not nail it when she leads her teammates into Glasgow's Hydro for the opening ceremony tomorrow morning (AEST).

"I'll be thinking, 'Soak this in, soak this in, soak it in,' and then I won't soak it in," she said.

"That's what will happen."
