title: Serious corruption findings against senior ACT public servants
text:
A years' long ACT Integrity Commission investigation has made serious corruption findings against territory government officials.

The investigation surrounded the awarding of a multi-million-dollar tender for the refurbishment of Campbell Primary School between 2019 and 2020.

Former ACT Education Directorate director-general Katy Haire awarded the contract to Lendlease, despite a bid by local firm Manteena being deemed better and cheaper.

In public hearings, which began in 2023, a directorate official — assigned the pseudonym "John Green" — told the commission the Construction Forestry and Maritime Employees Union (CFMEU) didn't like the company because it didn't have a union agreement.

Mr Green alleged ACT Education Minister Yvette Berry's then-chief of staff, Joshua Ceramidas, had said "Manteena is not to get it," citing the company's "industrial relations performance".

The commission was critical of Mr Green's conduct, including dishonest evidence that he gave to the auditor-general, but found he should be "regarded as a whistleblower" and his conduct did not amount to serious corrupt conduct.

'Calculated attempt to cover up'

The commission found Mr Ceramidas and Ms Haire "dishonestly provided a back door" by which the strategic interests of the CFMEU were given priority.

Former ACT Education Directorate director-general Katy Haire. (Supplied: ACT Education Directorate)

"So far as Ms Haire and Mr Ceramidas is each concerned, there can be no doubt that the corrupt conduct which they have been found to have committed was of a character that would be likely to threaten public confidence in the integrity of government or public administration," the report said.

"They dishonestly provided a back door by which the strategic interests of the CFMEU were given priority over, firstly, the actual policy of the government and the interests of the territory community.

"Not only was the process itself improperly skewed, resulting in manifest unfairness and also, as it happened, substantially increased cost, but both Ms Haire and Mr Ceramidas undertook a calculated attempt to cover up the truth of what occurred.

"Accordingly, the corrupt conduct of each amounted to serious corrupt conduct."

In a statement, Mr Ceramidas rejected the commission's findings.



"I reject the findings made by the commission and regard its conduct throughout the investigation as requiring external scrutiny," the statement read.

"I am reviewing the report and not in a position to comment further."

The ABC has sought comment from Ms Haire.

ACT Education Minister Yvette Berry had no adverse findings made against her. (ABC News: Greg Nelson)

During the public hearings, Ms Berry had told the commission she was aware the CFMEU "were unhappy with Manteena" but denied being aware of the specifics or having told her staff the company shouldn't get the contract.

"I just don't think it's something I would say," Ms Berry said.

The union's then national and local secretary, Zach Smith, also denied having raised Manteena's tender for the school with Ms Berry or her former chief of staff.

The report made no findings on Ms Berry's conduct.

The commission's investigation also sparked several court proceedings.

In May 2024, Ms Haire went to the Supreme Court alleging an apprehension of bias, saying the ACT Integrity Commission's head Michael Adams had refused to allow the cross-examination of Mr Green.

And, in July 2024, Mr Cerimidas unsuccessfully sought an injunction on the commission's intention to hold public hearings.

Calls for Berry's resignation

Canberra Liberals leader Mark Parton has called on ACT Education Minister Yvette Berry to resign. (ABC News: Joel Wilson)

Canberra Liberals leader Mark Parton said if he was chief minister, he would strip Ms Berry of the education portfolio.

"I think it's very clear today that the minister for education needs to resign … and if she chooses not to resign, I would be calling on the chief minister to dismiss her," Mr Parton said.

"If indeed the minister was not aware about decisions that were being made by her chief of staff and the director-general, well I think that says a lot about how the minister runs her portfolios."

He described the ACT government as a "crisis" and said such incidents had contextualised the now-abandoned prospective coalition of the Canberra Liberals and ACT Greens.

"What we're seeing here is that if you have the same government in power for 26 years, you're going to get this sort of outcome.

" There needs to be a broom run though this place. "

Ms Berry's office is expected to issue a statement later today.

title: Why netball is growing into a sport of choice for men and boys
text:
When Daniel Statton was plotting a move from Far North Queensland to Geelong, the prospect of playing more competitive netball was part of the attraction.

The 19-year-old is one of a growing number of Australian men and boys taking up the sport.

He has now been selected for Australia's national men's netball team, the Kelpies.

"I'm just still hungry for more," he said.

"There's still more to achieve in netball, and I'm just wanting to get after it."

Daniel Statton looked forward to more competitive netball after moving to Geelong from Cairns. (ABC News: Jonathon Kendall)

Statton has represented Australia at under-18 level and played for Queensland in the Australian Mixed and Men's Netball Association national championships in Perth earlier this year.

"I'm pretty athletic and agile, so the coaches love that,' he said.

" I always love to get up and get a bit of an aerial ball and get the crowd going a little bit. "

Born and raised in Cairns, Statton was the only boy playing netball in his sister's teams until he was about 12.

After that, age restrictions for boys forced him to switch to football and basketball.

Lynton McKay helped found the Cairns men's netball league. (ABC News: Bridget Herrmann)

He was not able to return to the game outside of mixed competitions until he was about 16.

"It was really exciting to know there were more like-minded athletes who enjoyed playing netball," he said.

Taking a shot

Before moving to Victoria for further study, Statton played in the Cairns men's netball league, one of the only men's competitions in Queensland.

Now in its third season, the Cairns competition has grown to four teams.

Lynton McKay grew up playing basketball, but after trying netball more than a decade ago, he never looked back.

Daniel Statton has represented his state and country in netball. (ABC News: Jonathon Kendall)

He founded the Cairns men's competition so he could continue playing the sport after moving back to the region from Melbourne.

"There wasn't really anything on the scene other than mixed indoor [netball]," he said.

He said once men and boys had the netball itch, the sport was hard to give up.

"It's not as easy as it looks; it's quite tactical," he said.

Lots of talent emerging

Mr McKay said one of the biggest challenges to growing netball in regional areas was the smaller pool of players with competition from many other sports.

He said there was still a stigma about netball being a female sport, "probably more so in a regional place than a big city like Melbourne or Brisbane".

"But I do think that stigma is dropping," he said.

The popularity of netball among boys and men in Australia is growing. (Supplied: Queensland Suns)

"I'm also a teacher, and I do find there's huge participation now among boys. They all seem to really enjoy netball when they come down and give it a try."

Mr McKay said the Cairns men's competition was "still a work in progress" but said the region had some "really talented players", including some recent high school graduates who had represented their state.

"Considering we've only got four teams, it's a testament to the talent we have here in Cairns," he said.

Leprechaun Netball Club men's team co-captain Damian Nowak said the sport was a safer option for people's joints.

He said the social aspect was also a big drawcard.

Netball has to compete with other sports such as football and basketball for male talent. (Supplied: Clusterpix)

"We're just down here to have fun, a good bunch of mates, a good bunch of dads," Mr Nowak said.

Mr McKay said the best way for netball competitions to attract more players was to "keep pushing".

"I've noticed when we have school carnivals there are a lot of schools now starting to enter boys' teams," he said.

"This didn't happen five years ago."

title: Indigenous-owned town hopes giant fish statue will attract tourists
text:
Visitation to the remote Indigenous community of Yalata on the far-west coast of South Australia has been limited since it was established in 1952.

The community is an Indigenous Protected Area owned and managed by the Yalata Anangu Aboriginal Corporation.

"We still live in a cultured life, a traditional lifestyle," said Uncle Fabian Peel, who has lived in Yalata since he was 13 years old.

The community consists largely of Anangu traditional owners who were forcibly relocated from their ancestral homelands in the lead-up to British nuclear bomb testing in Maralinga in the 1950s.

A sign in Yalata, an Aboriginal community located 100km west of Ceduna on the edge of the Nullarbor Plain. (ABC News: Stephen Opie)

Increasingly, the coastline around Yalata was being used by people, including international tourists, trying to fish off the Great Australian Bight coastline, Uncle Peel said

"We hid our fishing spot away from the public for years. But people keep coming here," he said.

" We turned around and said, 'Why don't we open it to the public? We need money.' "

Fabian Peel (left) was at the unveiling of the Mighty Mulloway sculpture. (Supplied: Michael Roxas)

Anglers fishing near Yalata have been known to catch monster mulloway, gummy shark, Australian salmon, and snapper.

While visitation to Yalata is still restricted to the public – and alcohol strictly prohibited – the community has decided to promote its camping and fishing spots through two new public artworks: a Yalata Roadhouse sign and the bronze Mighty Mulloway.

The two-metre, 200-kilogram bronze mulloway sculpture was purchased by Yalata Anangu Community and unveiled this month at the Yalata Roadhouse, which is also owned and managed by the community.

Two public artworks were purchased by Yalata Anangu Aboriginal Corporation, including a tourist road sign. (Supplied: Michael Roxas)

"We came up with the idea of putting a statue of the Mulloway … and opened up fishing to the public," said Uncle Peel.

He hoped the Mighty Mulloway, also known as the "silver ghost" fish, would entice people to apply to visit Yalata and learn about the diversity of the lands and the people who live there.

" I want to see people from out of community get involved with us but the main thing is I want to see our young ones get employment through the program. "

He said visitation to Yalata would result in long-term, stable employment in retail and rangers to protect the campgrounds.

David White says the Yalata community had not ventured into tourism before. (ABC News: Stephen Opie)

"The community is closed, but not 100 per cent closed," explained David White, Yalata Anangu Community chief executive.

"We have extensive camping and fishing along the coast that's available for visitors with permits."

While the plunge into tourism has been in the pipeline for almost a decade, Mr White said the sculpture was Yalata's "first real step".

"They want to be a true part of the Eyre Peninsula and share their culture and beliefs to be part of the Far West Coast region," he said.

"Kimba has the Big Galah, Nullarbor Roadhouse has the Big Whale, and now we've got our bronzed mulloway, the Mighty Mulloway."

Reckoning with history

Yalata's history centres on the displacement of Aboriginal people from their ancestral homelands due to British nuclear tests at Maralinga in the 1950s.

"They're displaced people," Mr White said.

"But they're starting to really put their roots into the ground now."

Community gathered at Yalata Roadhouse when the two public artworks were installed. (Supplied: Michael Roxas)

Uncle Peel said Yalata had changed a lot in recent years.

"Twenty years ago we didn't have any houses here. Some of us still lived out in the bush in humpies," he said.

" We've been put in the dark and had to rely on government funding for all the years. We want to make something for ourselves to say that it belongs to us. "

Mr White said it had been a massive move for the community, but people were finally making Yalata their home.

A spokesperson for the South Australian Tourism Commission said statues and public art were great ways to make travellers pull over and spend time and money in towns "that they might otherwise drive straight past".

The 2-metre-long stainless steel mulloway was made by Port Lincoln artist Ken Martin. (Supplied)

Uncle Peel hoped Yalata's step into tourism would create more opportunities for its young people while helping the town retain its agency.

"It's the young ones that are going to run the community soon and looking after us," he said.

"We want to grow. But we want to control that."

title: Record $60k fine for man behind 'brazen' dump site that will cost $1m to clean
text:
The owner of what has been described as one of Victoria's worst illegal dump sites has been handed a record fine of $60,000 and ordered to clean up the polluted property in Melbourne's outer north-east.

Joseph Chetcuti pleaded guilty to depositing industrial waste without authorisation and failing to comply with an environmental action notice at the Heidelberg Magistrates' Court on Tuesday.

The state's Environment Protection Authority (EPA) said the Diamond Creek property was brought to its attention in May 2025, after fire crews were called to the site only to discover industrial waste being burnt off without authorisation.

Emergency services responded to a fire on a semi-rural property last year. (Supplied: Environment Protection Authority Victoria)

An investigation found the fire involved construction and demolition waste, along with asbestos found within the soil on the property.

"This was a brazen and highly organised criminal waste dumping operation," EPA chief executive officer Joss Crawford said.

Surveillance and aerial photography by the environmental regulator revealed trucks carrying fill material transported the industrial waste to the site.

Satellite images show what appears to be the site of a large-scale dump at the Diamond Creek property. (Supplied: Google Earth)

The EPA said Chetcuti failed to comply with notices issued and are now undertaking further investigations into the nature of the trucks used to transport the waste.

"Our investigations have identified multiple trucking companies who we believe used Chetcuti's site as a dumping ground to avoid the cost of lawful disposal," Ms Crawford said.

" The community is rightly outraged by his actions. "

The fine is believed to be the largest issued to an individual under the Victorian Environment Protection Act.

On top of the fine, the EPA said Chetcuti was required to clean up the property at an estimated cost surpassing $1 million.

Reports of illegal dumping have been on the rise across Melbourne in recent years. (Supplied: Michael Oldfield)

The prosecution comes amid a rise in household and industrial waste being dumped across suburban Melbourne.

Last October, the Victorian state government announced $21 million worth of funding to tackle illegal dumping incidents, including a task force to investigate and prosecute fly-tippers and a rebate program to support councils with clean up efforts.

The state government also promised the "naming and shaming" of offenders caught dumping waste illegally.

Hume City Council, in Melbourne's north-west, has been calling out sanctioned businesses on their Facebook page, including a business fined over $7,000 earlier this month.

title: Sharks 'happy to take underdog tag' ahead of blockbuster World Cup
text:
Australia's men's water polo team are set to take on some of the biggest teams on the planet this week at the World Aquatics Water Polo World Cup Finals.

The Sharks get their campaign underway on Thursday evening against the most daunting of opponents, reigning world champions Spain.

For most, the sight of the world champions lining up poolside at the Sydney Olympic Park Aquatic Centre would send shivers down your spine.

The Sharks, though, are not familiar with being daunted by anyone.

"I think if you were to ask anyone else in the world outside of our group, they would call us underdogs," goalkeeper Nic Porter told ABC Sport during the Sharks' pre-tournament training camp on the Gold Coast.

"But I think you ask any one of us in our group, we'd give you a very different answer.

"As Australians, as we all know, we're more than happy to take the underdog tag. I actually think Australians relish that.

"We'll take the underdog tag, that's fine, we understand where we're ranked in the world.

"But I also remember where we were ranked in the world in Paris 2024 and what happened there."

Paris run and quarterfinal pain fuelling drive for LA

Marcus Berehulak played when the Aussies stunned Serbia at the Olympics. (Getty Images: Clive Rose)

Ah yes, the Paris Olympics. There, the Sharks made a pledge to stun the world and, by and large, they did just that.

Eventual gold medallists Serbia were beaten by the staggering scoreline of 8-3 in the group stages.

"The way we approached the game against Serbia was, we had no fear," Porter said.

"And I think we must do the same in this tournament.

"The key for us and everything that we're focusing on now is those first four minutes against Spain.

"Dominate those first four minutes and then let's have a look at the rest of the game after that.

"I think if we can start really hot with a home crowd backing us, supporting us, giving us a bit of extra energy, I think it's going to be a really difficult night for the Spanish.

"And we're certainly going to be doing everything we can to make it as difficult as possible for them."

Nic Porter is relishing playing in front of a home crowd. (Supplied)

The Serbia game was not even the high point for Australia in Paris.

Hungary, the nine-time champions and most successful nation in the history of men's water polo at the Olympics, and hosts France were also beaten, by identical 9-8 scorelines.

The world could safely be considered to have been suitably shocked, especially the French, who were backed by an enormously parochial crowd at the Paris Aquatic Centre.

"That was a special night, the French night," coach Tim Hamill recalls.

"Among others, that was really special."

How the Aussies silenced the French crowd The Australian men's water polo team is hell-bent on riding a wave of good form all the way to the knockout rounds, after a hard-fought, dramatic 9-8 victory over hosts France and their legions of fans.

Tim Putt, who has returned to the squad after narrowly missing out in Paris, noted that the victory against the French was a real litmus test on how far the Australians had come in a relatively short space of time.

"For that Paris cycle, one of the great metrics I look at is France," the 27-year-old said.

"France was always our crossover game where we had to beat them to go through in the tournament and for like the first three tournaments we always just lost.

"But we all knew we could still beat them and we were gonna beat them next time.

"We had an official training match against them at the start of the Paris tour and we lost I think and then, leading into the Olympics, we were playing really good games, we came second at a tournament in Serbia and in the Olympics we had France as our last group game.

"I thought, 'Oh, they're going to pump them, they're going to absolutely pump them,' and they did.

"It took three years to show on the scoreboard what we were doing, but it did show in the end."

Australia's win over France was a thriller in Paris. (Getty Images: Clive Rose)

Alas, that victory against the French was the pinnacle of the Games for the Aussies, who fell in a heartbreaking quarterfinal defeat to the USA on penalties.

"That was the one, that's the heartbreaker," Hamill said.

"That's the one that we still lose a little bit of sleep over — and I say that only half jokingly.

"It was a huge moment for us that we ultimately didn't grab to its full potential at the time, but we got huge learnings out of that.

"We were one shot away from playing for Olympic medal, but we've learned from that, we've taken a huge number of lessons from that and we intend to use those for Sydney World Cup and as we get close to the LA Olympic Games."

'The next big Everest'

Tim Hamill has enjoyed the home build-up ahead of the World Cup. (Supplied: Water Polo Australia)

Hamill has been coach of the Sharks since 2021, taking over from Croatian international Elvis Fatović after the Tokyo Games.

The quarterfinal appearance at the 2024 Olympics was a spectacular high point of his tenure so far.

However, Hamill was clear that he only sees Australia improving on the way to Los Angeles in 2028, and beyond to the Brisbane 2032 Games.

"We know that on our day we can beat any team in the world, but doing it consistently across a tournament like the World Cup in Sydney or the upcoming Olympic Games is the next big step for us, that's probably the next big Everest for this group," Hamill said.

"The Sydney World Cup provides a great opportunity for some really high-level international exposure and we'll back-end our investment to make sure that 2027 and 2028 are heavily towards getting enough games.

"We're a long way away from the home of water polo [southern Europe], so we've got to get over there and get that international dosage over the next couple of years."

The Sharks were left gutted after their quarterfinal defeat against the USA in a shootout. (Getty Images: Quinn Rooney)

Skipper Nathan Power, who played a key role in the Sharks' run to the Olympic quarterfinals in Paris, noted that the time to switch on and take the next step was now.

"I think as a country, we've always done a really good job of being up and ready to go when it gets to a major tournament," Power said.

"But if we want to be, I guess, in Olympic terms, taken seriously as a second-week team, it really is a matter of starting to string together these results into a complete tournament.

"For us, there's a mental resilience thing there. While we can lean into the joys we had from Paris, we can also lean into the heartbreak we had there too.

"The stage we're getting to here [at the World Cup] starts at the quarterfinal stage and the quarterfinals is where we fell down in Paris, so even that in itself is a good pick-up point to start and coming forward with a positive result this time round."

The Sydney World Cup offers a fantastic opportunity for Australia to compete on home soil and use a home advantage that is rarely available to them.

Australian fans often have to travel. (Getty Images: DeFodi Images/Marcel ter Bals)

But Hamill noted that it's not just about getting results at this tournament, but continuing to build.

"High-performance sport and Olympic sport will always be about outcomes," Hamill said.

"We haven't hidden from the fact that what we want to achieve in LA is significant, we want it to be more than Paris.

"But for us as a group, there is more to it than that.

"This program has struggled for a number of years to get some traction in sport, so having a stand full at the Sydney World Cup, with young boys and young girls and families and inspiring the next generation and legacy and program depth, they're all really, really important for this team.

"The players themselves talk about leaving the cap in a better place moving forward, so legacy is a word that's come a lot through this group.

"So yes, we want to achieve what we want to achieve in Sydney World Cup and LA and hopefully beyond.

"But we're trying to build something long-term and sustainable with this water polo program in this country."

Sharks call on crowd to bring the passion

Nic Porter is known for wearing his heart on his sleeve. (Getty Images: Clive Rose)

Ticket sales for the World Cup have been hugely promising according to Water Polo Australia, setting the stage for what should be an electric atmosphere poolside over the next five days.

"My concern is that a lot of it's going to be for the Greeks and the Hungarians and the other countries," Hamill said, half-jokingly.

He probably has cause to be concerned.

Water polo is a huge deal in Europe and by all accounts, the expat communities of those nations in Sydney are set to really embrace the tournament as a rare chance to see their heroes in the flesh.

"That's probably the thing that we're probably the most excited for," Porter said.

"For us, when we go overseas and we play against these European countries or even America or Japan or wherever we play, we've always faced such a passionate home crowd that are cheering against us, and that can be pretty daunting sometimes, particularly when you go into some of these European nations where water polo is the number one sport.

"It can be very hostile there sometimes. So for us, we're hoping to create a bit of a hostile environment for our opponents.

Marcus Berehulak and Nic Porter are thrilled to be playing at home in Sydney. (Getty Images: Clive Rose)

"We want to have some deafening support, we want the classic Aussie spirit on display in the stands because we really feel like that'll help push the men's and women's teams over the line.

"We really can't wait to see a sea of green and gold in the stands, Australians love to get rowdy in a respectful way and support the athletes, and we can't wait to see that transferred over to water polo in a couple of weeks."

Marcus Berehulak noted that although crowds in major events such as the LEN Champions League Final Four were "hectic," that energy could be beneficial to everyone.

"It's pretty crazy, some of those events, especially Final Four, you've got flares and banners and everything chucked into the pool and whatnot in the end," he said.

"It's definitely a hostile environment."

Hamill noted that Porter, who played in Greece for Panionios last year and has just signed for French powerhouse Marseille, is one player who relishes a frenetic atmosphere.

"Nic's a really great example of utilising energy from the crowd and family support to make you perform, he wears his heart on his sleeve.

"It's not for everyone, playing up to the crowd, but the tournament that he put together at Paris was, from a goalkeeping point of view, as good as you'll see and that reflected in our results."

'Underdog' Sharks aim to use the home support

The Sharks feel like they are coming in under the radar. (Getty Images: Adam Pretty)

Hamill was aware that all the players were pumped about playing on home soil.

"Sharing those [ticket sale] numbers with the players, you can see the excitement on their faces," Hamill said.

"Knowing that we're going to have big crowds at home is really exciting for everyone.

"There's often a desire for teams to not get caught up in that emotion and the hype, but often being very much the underdog team that we are, including like we were in Paris, I think of it a little bit differently in terms of finding moments, opportunities.

"We can utilise that energy and crowd noise and not avoid it.

"Everyone talks about ignoring the distractions of the Olympic Games, but I'm more about embracing those so-called distractions and how they can provide momentum for teams, which is something I think we did incredibly well in Paris and we intend to do the same in Sydney.

"We won't avoid the distractions, we won't avoid the people and moments, we'll open our doors to all of that."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Power agreed, noting that embracing the pressure had worked well for the team at the Olympics.

"It's something which as a team we've consciously been trying to do a lot more," Power said.

"In the lead into Paris and beyond there was a 'smell the roses' and take a moment to appreciate those very cool things that you get to experience.

"Some people might view it as a distraction. Instead, you're acknowledging that thing is there and then you're using that to have a positive impact on you.

"It would be silly to go, 'Oh, we're not going to think about it too much,' we're going to be in a crowd full of people cheering for us, which is something very special.

"We've had some really wonderful experiences with the crowds in Brisbane and Perth and now in Sydney, to get a chance to really turn it on in a very iconic venue for Australian sport too.

"It's certainly not lost on us how important that'll be."

title: Historical journals give man past after he spent years making one up
text:
For most of his life, Cam Sojan never knew his family history.

He knew his father was born in Geraldton, went to school in Perth and then joined the military in Canberra, but learned little else before his father passed away.

"Sadly I think my father carried a bit of shame about his origin through his whole life," Mr Sojan said.

"I'm 55 and up until 54 I had no idea where I came from.

" I had all sorts of stories I had confected about who I was and where I came from but I didn't really know. "

Loading...

In 2025, during a period of soul-searching, Mr Sojan stumbled upon the Irwin District Historical Society, a dedicated group of residents in Dongara helping preserve the Midwest's past.

The Victorian man, who now lives in Sydney, soon realised there was a whole bloodline of Sikh men with his surname who had arrived in Dongara in the 1900s.

Several Sikh journals then revealed Mr Sojan's family story.

Cam Sojan is a fourth-generation descendant of Sojan Singh (pictured left), who moved to Dongara in the 19th century. (Supplied: Sikh Association of WA)

"I am directly related to Sojan Singh who was the Sikh Indian gentleman who arrived in the late 19th century when there was a wave of Sikh and Afghans who arrived at that time," he said.

"[The historical society] had all of this information about my great-grandfather and a little bit about my grandfather, who I absolutely had never met.

" That's when I decided: 'I've got to come to Western Australia and discover this for myself.' "

Mr Sojan travelled to WA with his wife, Allison, to learn more about his history. (Supplied: Cam Sojan)

Travelling for connection

Mr Sojan travelled across the country to Perth to meet long-lost relatives, including cousins and an uncle.

He said initial nerves at meeting his uncle in Geraldton, 420 kilometres north of Perth, dissolved when he was welcomed into their family home with open arms.

The family got together several times during his stay.

"We met with their son and his family and just sat around and shared stories, the good, bad, the ugly, which is what families are," he said.

Cam Sojan (left), his wife Allison and his uncle Roy (right) with his wife Jeanie at Sikh Lane in the Shire of Irwin. (Supplied: Cam Sojan)

"My connection to the land and connection to the people couldn't be stronger."

Back home, Mr Sojan said his heart finally felt full.

" I'm centred and grounded by knowing where I come from and what my purpose is. "

"I'm so proud … it [makes me want] to really fight the fight of multiculturalism and embrace the heritage of multiculturalism in Australia," he said.

Cam Sojan (third from the right) with relatives in Geraldton. (Supplied: Cam Sojan)

Darker side of history

Mr Sojan thinks the secrecy surrounding his family history highlights the darker side of Australia's migration history.

"The wave of the White Australia policy really took hold of these interracial children who were half-Irish, half-Indian and were subject to the difficulties of growing up in Australia," he said.

Mr Sojan said his father, like many immigrants, suffered bullying in childhood.

"My father was quite elitist but I think that's because he was like, 'you've got to be the best'," he said.

"He really chose to look forward and suppress the dark shadows of his early childhood."

Dongara, 350 kilometres north of Perth, has a rich multicultural history. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Chris Lewis)

The power of objects

Irwin District Historical Society secretary, Trish Parker, said Mr Sojan's story made the work her team did "worth it".

"To think that we've been a small part in putting Cam's story together for him; it's an enormous honour," she said.

"You do a lot of work behind the scenes … conserving objects and cataloguing them.

" I think it makes you even more determined to keep doing a good job because this work we do in conserving and telling stories, it does make a difference. "

Trish Parker says it has been an honour to help Mr Sojan learn his family history. (ABC News: Piper Duffy)

Ms Parker said working with the society had made her realise the important role objects played in people's histories.

"Our collection is so big and there are so many stories in there," she said.

"It's really taught me the importance of looking after those objects because of the stories they tell, not just for their family but for the community as well."

title: Tourism body criticises 'disgusting price gouging' for beef expo beds
text:
Daily charter flights to Australia's biggest beef expo are becoming a "reasonable option" for attendees, as an accommodation shortage grips a regional city also chosen to host events for the 2032 Olympic Games.

The Central Queensland city of Rockhampton is home to the triannual Beef Australia industry showcase and has also been selected to host rowing and canoe sprint events on the Fitzroy River for the 2032 Brisbane Olympic and Paralympic Games.

The week-long beef expo attracts about 55,000 people who visit the event multiple times during the week.

Organisers provide hundreds of temporary campsites near the showgrounds to help ease the accommodation issue.

Beef Australia is the largest beef industry event in the Southern Hemisphere. (Supplied: Beef Australia)

Tourism body Capricorn Enterprise said 1,200 short-term accommodation rooms would be needed in the Rockhampton and Capricorn Coast region by 2032 to meet current and future demand.

It said the region had 2,500 commercial rooms offering 4,600 beds.

The last major hotel built in the region opened a decade ago.

Beef Australia CEO Simon Irwin said accommodation was a major challenge for its upcoming expo in May 2027, the largest beef industry event in the Southern Hemisphere.

About 55,000 visitors attend the event in Rockhampton. (Supplied: Beef Australia)

"We are working with air charter operators to bring people down from Townsville, up from Toowoomba and in from Longreach just for day trips," Mr Irwin said.

" Because now, chartering a plane due to the scarcity and the cost of accommodation is a quite reasonable option for a lot of people. "

'Price gouging disgraceful'

Some apartments and homes in Rockhampton are currently being advertised for rent for $24,000 for the week of the beef expo, while some properties at nearby Yeppoon are advertised for $13,000.

"People get carried away. They see something up for $40,000 for the week and the reason you're seeing it is no-one has taken it," Mr Irwin said.

Capricorn Enterprise CEO Mary Carroll said the "price gouging" of some accommodation properties for the event was "disgraceful".

"[It's] jeopardising the event as well as the reputation of our city and greater region," she said.

" A premium price during a major event is expected and accepted, but not three times the normal rate and higher. "

Some accommodation in Rockhampton is being advertised for rent for $24,000 for the week of the beef expo. (Supplied: Beef Australia)

'Consistent' demand for rooms

About 15,000 spectators are expected to come to the city for the Olympics.

Rockhampton Mayor Tony Williams said he hoped developers and hoteliers would see the 2032 Games as a "great opportunity to invest".

"We need an investor to come in and build the infrastructure … there is a consistent demand there," he said.

A new Athlete Village for the Olympics is proposed to be built at the Rockhampton Railyards, with works still in the planning and approvals stage.

Rowers head out for an early morning training session on the Fitzroy River in Rockhampton. (ABC: Alice Roberts)

Cr Williams said although he was unaware of the buyer's plans, it was encouraging to see the recent $5.75 million sale of Rockhampton's rundown 66-room Plaza Hotel.

"Rather than seeing derelict buildings sit there idle and become issues, that investment is confidence that there is something happening," he said.

Ms Carroll said she was unaware of any concrete plans by Iwasaki Pty Ltd for the future of its derelict 331-room Capricorn Resort at Yeppoon, which closed in 2016.

"It's heartbreaking to see the deterioration of this once iconic resort, which could be subdivided from the 22,000-acre cattle property, for a hotel investor to develop into a world-class ecotourism development."

title: Prince George turns 13 as he heads to Eton
text:
Prince George, second in line to the throne, has turned 13 as he readies to follow in his father Prince William's footsteps and start at the prestigious Eton College.

Kensington Palace shared a picture of the prince as his grandfather, King Charles III, and Queen Camilla wished him a happy birthday.

As the eldest child of Prince William, heir to the British throne, and Princess Catherine, George is set to succeed his father as king one day.

Kensington Palace released this photo of Prince George on his 13th birthday. (Supplied)

But first, he will face another daunting challenge: the teen years and starting at Eton boarding school in September.

According to royal commentator Richard Fitzwilliams, the young prince is being "inducted into things very gradually", and as he grows older, he will increase his public appearances.

Prince George heading to Eton College Prince George will attend Eton College this September, following in the footsteps of his father, Prince William.

The prince was born on July 22, 2013, at St Mary's Hospital in London to a global media frenzy and public celebrations. His full name is George Alexander Louis.

He was quickly whisked away on his first royal tour in April 2014, when his parents visited New Zealand and Australia, where the eight-month-old was seen excitedly interacting with the wildlife.

William, Kate and George initially lived in a rented farmhouse in rural Anglesey off the coast of north-west Wales.

Shielded from cameras from an early age, the prince's rare public appearances as a toddler were exhaustively reported in the British media, calling him "Gorgeous George".

Prince William with baby George during an official visit to Australia in 2014. (Reuters: Chris Jackson)

Since then, his birthday celebrations have been kept private as well, with the family occasionally sharing official photographs.

"William and Catherine have done extremely well in trying to bring him up in a simple way, sort of, as normally as possible," Mr Fitzwilliams told AFP.

In 2017, the family moved to an apartment in London's Kensington Palace, and George had his first day of school at a private school in south London.

They later moved to their long-term home on the Windsor estate, south-west of London, where George has attended the private Lambrook School since 2022, alongside his siblings, Charlotte, 11, and Louis, the youngest at eight.

Football and helicopters

He has already been part of high-profile events, appearing alongside his family on the balcony of Buckingham Palace for the Trooping the Colour ceremony, and helping carry the train of his grandfather's robes during Charles's coronation in 2023.

Prince George alongside his sister Princess Charlotte at Trooping the Colour, the king's annual birthday parade, last month. (AP: Kirsty Wigglesworth)

Unlike Louis, who has been pictured comically pulling faces at events, George has appeared to be more serious and composed in public engagements.

"He was a bit shy in early years", said Mr Fitzwilliams, pointing out he has been seen being "helpful to Charlotte and Louis on occasions".

" If you look at him in the formal occasions, I think you can see him assuming a role reasonably easily. "

Beyond the royal title, Prince William has also passed on to George his love for football and his team, Aston Villa.

The prince often accompanies William to Villa games, and his mother to the Wimbledon tennis tournament.

Prince William and Prince George in the royal box watching the Wimbledon men's singles final earlier this month. (AP: Dave Shopland/Invision)

He is also said to have an interest in helicopters and could follow in the footsteps of his father, who has worked as an air ambulance pilot.

In September, George will begin boarding at Eton, but his family will be close by in Windsor.

"This is where he'll be, as it were, inducted into statecraft, history," Mr Fitzwilliams said.

Prince George with his family at a carol service at Westminster Abbey in December. (Reuters: Aaron Chown)

Like his father and uncle Harry, George will have security at the famous secondary school, which charges more than £63,000 ($120,000) in annual fees.

"Now he's a teenager, Eton beckons," Mr Fitzwilliams said.

"Eton is the big step towards maturity."

AFP

title: Victorian teachers 'need relief' as thousands take to the streets
text:
Thousands of people have taken to the streets across Melbourne's CBD, as teachers and education support staff walk off the job in their second major strike action of the year.

While state schools are open today, many could only provide supervision for a small number of students.

At the rally's peak, crowds disrupted traffic between Bourke Street and the state parliament.

It is the second major strike action this year, after roughly 35,000 people marched through the CBD in March as part of a 24-hour strike.

Protesters carried bright banners and signs through Melbourne's streets. (ABC News: Toby Hussey)

Meaghan Sinclair, who works at North Melbourne Primary School, said school staff felt as though the government did not value them.

"A lot of us are on the very bottom rung of payment; a lot of us are also dealing with a lot of kids with very complex needs from trauma to physical disability," the teaching assistant said.

"We're working with some of the most vulnerable kids in society in public schools. Is it really enough to be working minimum wage?"

Protesters had fun making their posters for the teacher strike. (774 ABC Melbourne: Kate Meade)

The strike comes after Australian Education Union (AEU) members voted to reject the state government's latest pay offer on the weekend.

The union said just over half of its members opposed the government's new offer, which this time included support staff in a 28 per cent pay increase over four years and halving the number of compulsory meetings educators must attend.

Instead, the union is calling for the state government to agree to a three-year deal and for more action to alleviate educators' workloads.

Some educators say they are at breaking point. (ABC News: Sacha Payne)

AEU Victoria President Justin Mullaly said workloads were a key issue for teachers.

"They work in the lowest-funded schools in the country, and that means that they carry a significant burden when it comes to workload," he told ABC Radio Melbourne.

"They're working on average 12 hours unpaid per week. They need some relief from that.

"That's one of the key things that we need to see further addressed in negotiations."

Teachers are demanding better working conditions and higher pay. (ABC News: Sacha Payne)

Mr Mullaly said the union's concerns were more about staff shortages and workloads than the government's pay offer.

"When it comes to pay, there's no doubt that a 28 per cent pay increase is significant. We've always acknowledged that," he said.

"But when you've got a situation where people are working in schools where shortages of teachers continue, you've got to do more to attract and, in particular, retain the workforce."

People are filling the streets of Melbourne's CBD amid industrial action. (ABC News: Sacha Payne)

While many children did not have to attend school today, some used their day off to support parents and teachers at the strike action.

Maggie, 10, said she believed her teachers deserved to be treated better.

"Our teachers are good … they need more pay and we want our school to be the best it can be," Maggie said.

"They care for us," fellow student Oscar said.

"They're not doing it for them, they're doing it for the kids."

Maggie, 10, and Oscar, 8, join the strike action in Melbourne. (ABC News)

Regional communities take action

Regional communities are also taking part in today's industrial action.

More than 150 teachers from Wodonga schools gathered in the middle of town and walked through the main street.

Many said the region was struggling to fill positions because teachers simply had the better option of working in Albury, a few kilometres across the border in NSW.

Rohan Barry says teachers are driving across the border to get better pay. (ABC News: Philippe Perez)

Wodonga Senior College teacher Rohan Barry said it had been a major issue at his school.

"There's a relief teacher I know who can work for four days in New South Wales and earn slightly more money than five days in Victoria, and that's partially addressed with the current proposal, but not fully," he said.

"Teachers will be looking just across the border for other solutions.

"As Victorian teachers, we would rather that that's not even an issue."

Wodonga Middle Years College teacher Cameron Middleton said as well as pay, more general funding allocated to improve schools for the region was needed.

"We're known as the Education State, but with the lack of funding we have, I don't know how we can be proud to be calling ourselves [that], because at the moment, we're not," he said.

Cameron Middleton says funding to improve schools is needed. (ABC News: Philippe Perez)

'Disappointing' disruptions, government says

Deputy Premier and Minister for Education Ben Carroll said discussions with the union were continuing.

"Families are already juggling a lot, and today's disruption is disappointing," he said.

"This deal would have made Victorian teachers and education support staff the best paid in the country together with the best conditions.

"This was a close vote, and we will continue to work with the AEU to resolve this matter."

Victorian teachers say they are overworked and undervalued. (ABC News: Sacha Payne)

In June, Victorian teachers went against AEU advice and voted down a pay rise of between 28 and 32 per cent.

In March, teachers and supporters took statewide strike action, marching to parliament in central Melbourne, with police estimating 35,000 people were in attendance.

The March strike was the first of its kind since AEU members took statewide industrial action in 2013 when negotiations with the Baillieu Coalition government broke down.

title: Star footballer's protest over transfer leads to training cancellation
text:
Wolverhampton striker Tolu Arokodare allegedly forced the cancellation of a training session after he refused to leave the pitch.

Sources said the 25-year-old Nigerian sat down and demanded the club let him move to Italian club Fiorentina.

Wolves have rejected a bid from the Serie A side, having accepted an offer from Turkish club Trabzonspor.

Arokodare's actions led to new boss Cesar Peixoto terminating the session.

Arokodare will not be involved in Wolverhampton's friendly at Maidenhead United as Wolves continue their pre-season plans.

Arokodare denied the reports on social media.

"I was very surprised and disappointed to see the media reports today, many of which are inaccurate. Throughout my career, I have always taken great pride in conducting myself professionally and maintaining the highest training standards," he wrote on X.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"I remain under contract with Wolves, and my only intention is to fulfil my responsibilities professionally by training, doing my job, and contributing to the team in accordance with that contract."

The latest incident is another flashpoint in the forward's career at Wolves after he was disciplined in April following a confrontation with young forward Mateus Mane.

Arokodare signed a massive deal after leaving Genk before last season's EPL, but scored just three league goals as Wolves finished bottom of the Premier League and were relegated.

The incident presents an early challenge for Peixoto just weeks into his reign after he replaced Rob Edwards in a surprise move last month.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

title: Kiana started weightlifting after an injury. Now, she's a Comm Games athlete
text:
Kiana Elliott always knew she wanted to compete in the Olympics.

When she was aged just three, the Sydney 2000 Games captured her attention.

"There was a little seed planted in my head," she said.

She thought gymnastics would be her pathway to the podium, but injury meant she had to retire from the sport at 14.

She discovered weightlifting while looking for another way to keep fit.

"I didn't even know it [weightlifting] existed, and I found it fun," Elliott said.

"I pursued it, and I've kept going and I'm still going."

Kiana Elliott finds weightlifting fun. (ABC News: Sophie Holder)

Elliott has now represented Australia in weightlifting at multiple events, including several Junior World Championships, three Pacific Games and the pandemic-delayed Tokyo 2020 Olympic Games.

She competed at the Birmingham 2022 Games, having suffered an injury just two months earlier.

"There was a big question mark on whether I'd be able to go," she said.

"I lifted about 90-to-94 per cent of my best and that was good enough for fourth.

" That left a little bit of a fire in me. "

According to the Australian Commonwealth Games website, Elliott is among 27 South Australians expected to compete at Glasgow.

'Anything can happen'

Elliott said she was focused on being in a good headspace ahead of the Games.

"I personally don't push too hard in terms of maximal weight and maximal attempts in the gym because I need to keep myself physically intact for competition," she said.

"Sport is like a little microcosm of life, it's the stories of the underdog, the triumph, the adversities, it puts you in a situation where it counts right now, so anything can happen on the day."

Weightlifter Kiana Elliott will compete in the 63kg competition at Glasgow. (ABC News: Sophie Holder)

Elliott said she would compete in the 63kg class against some "really great" athletes and people she respected at Glasgow.

" I just hope that I can put my best foot forward and compete as best as I can. "

Progressing past setbacks

Elliott's physical preparation coach at the South Australian Sports Institute (SASI), Michael Nash, said a lot of work had been put into Elliott's training program.

Do you know an athlete competing at the Commonwealth Games? Share with us Whether you are you a family member, a friend, a colleague or a coach, share your connection with us and how you will be supporting them.

"That works with, not only what she's previously had with her injuries [but] then building that into the Comm Games as well," he said.

"[We] feel good about [how] she's progressing. She's had a few setbacks with injuries as well, so my main priority with her is to look at what she's been doing and adjusting her sessions to make sure she's getting the most out of [them] so she can perform as well as she can."

He said Elliott was one of the most "technically good" weightlifters he had seen in Australia.

Michael Nash says Kiana Elliott is one of the most "technically good" weightlifters he has seen in Australia. (ABC News: Sophie Holder)

'Geez, what's happening here?'

Elliott said more women and girls had taken up weightlifting since she first started in the sport 13 years ago.

"That's super exciting," she said.

"Maybe I've played a little bit of a part of making that visible, locally I know some other athletes that made [it] visible for me.

"There's nothing better than pursuing your own strength and pursuing your own capability.

"It's in a gym environment, gym environments are very masculine, very ego-based and there's something … amusing, fun, cool … to be in the gym as a female, and the guys are like 'geez, what's happening here?'"

Elliott described weightlifting as fun and simple.

"It's a numbers game so it's really, really easy to measure your progress and its addicting for that," she said.

Kiana Elliott fits her weightlifting training around her work commitments. (ABC News)

Elliott said she fitted training around her job at SA Pathology, where she worked part-time.

"I don't know about balance, it's a bit off-kilter," she said.

"I'm really lucky to have built a really good relationship with my employer. The people that work close to me, they understand what I'm trying to do."

She said the general public tended to believe athletes who represent their country had everything handed to them.

"And you're set up and you're ready to go, and it's really not the case," Elliott said.

"I feel really lucky, proud and happy to have a good bunch of people around me and that include the guys here at SASI.

"I train here at South Australia Sports Institute, just outside of the CBD in Adelaide.

"I live down by the airport, I work in the CBD, if things weren't so close together, I couldn't make it work."

title: Queensland cattle farmers eye $100m move to crops
text:
As floodwaters moved across North West Queensland earlier this year, Lucas Findley was already preparing to plant crops.

His outback property, north of Julia Creek, sits in country better known for cattle than cotton.

In January, about 850 millimetres of rain fell there in a single week as widespread flooding devastated cattle properties across the region.

Yet within seven days of the last rain falling, machinery was back in paddocks, protected by levee banks, while floodwaters continued to move across the landscape.

Tony Perrett (right) visited Etta Plains Station earlier this year to hear about the region's push to diversify agriculture. (Supplied: Office of Minister Tony Perrett)

Etta Plains Station emerged with soaked soil, full water storages and water available for irrigation through the dry season.

Before the end of the month, Mr Findley had finished planting almost 5,000 hectares of cotton.

Mr Findley, who heads Gulf Cotton, said the experience backed an argument he has been making for years.

Producers needed different options for their land, and not just because it was good for the bank balance.

Lucas Findley says cropping in North West Queensland is ready to expand. (Supplied: Minister Ann Leahy's Office)

"It's my belief it is the only way to flood mitigate this region: to diversify your cattle business into cropping," he said.

Now, a $5.3 million investment could transform an emerging cropping sector into a much larger industry for the north west.

Ambitious targets for cattle country

Tony Perrett's visit to the North West earlier this year helped prompt the new research investment. (Supplied: Minister Tony Perrett's Office)

The Queensland government and cotton and grains industries said this week they would jointly fund research through the Unlocking Farming Systems for North Queensland project.

It aims to kickstart production of 100,000 bales of cotton and grow grain and pulse production to $100 million annually by 2035.

The project will initially focus on the Flinders catchment around Hughenden, Richmond and Julia Creek, before expanding into the Gilbert catchment further north.

Primary Industries Minister Tony Perrett said there would be more investment if cropping was proven to be viable over the longer term.

Putting the economics to the test

Richmond Shire Council expects its grain sorghum crop at Maxwelton to bring in about $500,000 this year. (ABC: Cam Lang)

About 200 kilometres east of Etta Plains, Richmond Shire Council has been testing whether broadacre cropping would work commercially in cattle country.

A Maxwelton chickpea crop harvested by the council in 2025 returned about $300,000 after costs, a return 10 times higher than running cattle over the same area.

Richmond Shire Mayor John Wharton said buyers had already been found for this year's crop, grain sorghum, and it would likely bring in about $500,000.

John Wharton with the council's grain sorghum crop at Maxwelton. (ABC Landline: Cam Lang)

"We're very pleased with the results," he said.

He believed the government's $100 million grain and pulse target was achievable.

"That could happen easily," he said.

" The land is there. The water's there. "

Cr Wharton said developing crop varieties suited to the area could mean more certainty for producers facing unpredictable wet seasons.

From crops to an industry

Mr Findley said the next step would be the ability to process crops locally, particularly cotton.

The scale of that investment can be significant.

A cotton gin that opened in Katherine in 2023 cost $70 million and can process up to 200,000 bales a year.

"A cotton gin is the main enabler for this region," he said.

" It's a chicken before the egg thing … but we need to somehow fund it, get it up and running and then move forward. "

Mr Perrett stopped short of committing government funding to a gin, but said local processing would bring broader economic benefits.

Water key to growth

Lucas Findley (left) with Minister Ann Leahy and Assistant Minister Sean Dillon at Etta Plains Station, north of Julia Creek. (Supplied: Minister Ann Leahy's Office )

While dryland cropping, which relies on rainfall and moisture in the soil rather than irrigation, is proving successful around Richmond, access to secure water remains a challenge for expanding irrigated agriculture.

Richmond Shire Council has since shifted its focus to dryland cropping, although Cr Wharton said irrigated farming remained important for the wider industry.

The state government is reviewing the Gulf Water Plan and undertaking the Gulf Regional Water Assessment, examining future water demand and supply options.

Draft findings are out for consultation.

Where cropping meets cattle country

Inspecting the Maxwelton crop and looking to the future of farming in the North West. (ABC Landline: Cam Lang)

For Mr Findley, expanding cropping was not about replacing the North West's generational cattle sector, but building a new sector alongside it.

Around Richmond, forage sorghum is already being grown to feed and finish cattle, while grain from the council's latest crop is destined for feedlots.

The change is visible on the Flinders Highway too.

Cr Wharton said 20 years ago grain trucks were rarely seen travelling the route.

"Now you see them every day," he said.

He said advances in farm machinery had also made large-scale cropping increasingly possible, allowing producers to plant large areas during short windows between monsoonal rain.

"The soils haven't changed, the weather hasn't changed, but the gear has."

“We act on the information we’re given from our medical team, the commentary around all of that, I probably push it to the side. title: Dalaroo geophysics unlocks thick Greenland rare earths potential
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Dalaroo geophysics unlocks thick Greenland rare earths potential Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Murray Ward July 23, 2026 — 12:58pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Dalaroo Metals says it has peered into the depths beneath the surface of its Blue Lagoon rare earths project in Greenland and liked what it saw, with a geophysical survey confirming unconsolidated sediment packages up to 50 metres in thickness. A total of twenty-one ground penetrating radar (GPR) survey lines were completed across five priority shoreline target areas, offering the first detailed, three-dimensional picture of the project’s sedimentary basin. Eastern sediment-filled valley interpreted as a source-to-sink transport pathway into the basin at Dalaroo’s Blue Lagoon project in Greenland. The work, combined with an echo sounder survey, has confirmed extensive sediment accumulations within the primary rare earth-bearing shoreline areas. The results are a significant step forward for Dalaroo’s sediment-hosted exploration model, which centres on rare earth-enriched alkaline granites eroding into a contained lagoon environment.

Advertisement

‘Every phase of exploration continues to strengthen our geological understanding of Blue Lagoon and increase our confidence.’ Dalaroo Metals chief executive officer John Morgan For these types of deposits, the thickness and continuity of the mineralised sediments are fundamental controls on their potential size. The survey demonstrated that sediment layers in several locations extend beyond the GPR system’s 50m penetration limit, leaving these deeper zones open. This suggests that even greater accumulations of valuable rare earth mineralisation might be lurking below. The company says one of the most significant outcomes was the identification of numerous previously unrecognised sediment-filled valleys extending beyond the current exploration footprint. Dalaroo believes these valleys represent additional “source-to-sink” transport pathways capable of carrying and concentrating rare earth-bearing heavy minerals from the surrounding source rocks.

Advertisement

The discovery lends considerable weight to the growing view that Blue Lagoon is part of a much bigger sedimentary system than originally recognised and extends well beyond the areas currently targeted by exploration. In addition to the GPR work, ten bathymetric survey lines were completed across the lagoon itself, delivering the first detailed model of the lagoon floor. This will help the company in interpreting sediment transport pathways and the depositional environments responsible for concentrating the valuable heavy mineral sands. Dalaroo Metals chief executive officer John Morgan said: “Our 2025 exploration program confirmed widespread rare earth mineralisation within shoreline sediments. This latest work now demonstrates that those sedimentary environments are substantially thicker than originally understood, with sediment accumulations extending to approximately 50 metres in several priority areas.” Whilst Greenland is shaping up to be the company’s headline act, Dalaroo is also building a solid portfolio of gold assets in West Africa’s highly endowed Birimian greenstone belt.

Advertisement

At its Bondoukou gold project in Côte d’Ivoire, recent soil sampling defined a significant 7km-long gold anomaly at the Gold Ridge prospect. The work returned a headline-grabbing peak soil value of 23.26 grams per tonne (g/t) gold and outlined multiple consistent anomalies that remain open along strike. The project sits in the Siguiri Basin, a prolific region that hosts several multi-million-ounce gold deposits. The company also holds the Kokoumbo project in Côte d’Ivoire, which came with a suite of historical drill hits that were never followed up, including a stellar 7.5-metre intersection grading 16g/t gold from surface and another hit of 4.5m at 4.22g/t gold. Back on home soil in Western Australia, the company holds the Lyons River project in the Gascoyne province, where it announced a gold system discovery in April 2024. With the geophysical fieldwork in Greenland now complete, Dalaroo says its focus will turn to integrating the new 3D model with its geological mapping and pending assay results from its recent comprehensive field program. This will allow management to prioritise future auger drilling, bulk sampling and metallurgical studies.

Advertisement

With its geological model at Blue Lagoon now significantly strengthened, Dalaroo has provided a strong technical foundation for the next phase of exploration. The GPR survey has essentially confirmed the “sink” is not only present, it is also substantially bigger and more complex than first thought. As Dalaroo works to integrate the new data and await key assay results, the market will likely be watching to see just how big this Greenlandic critical minerals system could become. title: Behind the glitter and excess lurks murder and savagery
text:
Sydney Opera House, July 21

It is the “curse” – la maledizione – that drives the drama of Verdi’s Rigoletto. We hear it in the overture’s first portentous notes and in Rigoletto’s despairing cry at the close. Rigoletto believes it ultimately derives from his ugliness, the hunchback that condemns him to a life of humiliation and corrosive bitterness, making him over-protective of the one thing of beauty in his life, his daughter, Gilda.

This classic production by the late Elijah Moshinsky transfers that ugliness to the world at large, in particular, the Duke of Mantua’s court, the centrepiece of designer Michael Yeargan’s rotating set. Creating a surprising sense of depth and space on the Joan Sutherland Theatre’s limited stage, it juxtaposes a palace of glittering debauchery, the false sanctuary of Rigoletto’s home and the seedy tavern of the final tragedy, all connected by murderous dark alleyways.

The Opera Australia Chorus evidently enjoy their debauchery in Rigoletto. Keith Saunders

For this revival, Dalibor Jenis sings the title role with a voice of strength and depth, grained with worry and fierce in self-loathing. He is impressive throughout, but never more so than in the crucial reconciliation scene with the seduced Gilda in Act 2 where impotent frustration over his fate mixes with fragile tenderness.

This was also Gemma Nha’s finest moment as Gilda. Here she displayed a wide emotional and tonal range, beginning Tutte le feste al tempio with winning quietness, before rising to thrilling passion. Earlier she had sung Caro Nome in Act 1 with a bright penetrating sound and a tight vibrato that crazed the tonal surface.

Her dramatic persona moved lightly between pertness, filial loyalty and youthful yearning. Kang Wang as the Duke also emphasised lightness rather than power, his attractive tenor sound and strong command of the upper range creating an autocrat of insouciant suaveness rather than overbearing menace.

La donna e mobile had buoyant clarity. Such a nice young man! With an effectively threatening edge to his low notes, David Parkin was an aptly brutal Sparafucile (literally “shoot gun” – you get the idea). As his seducing sister, Maddalena, Sian Sharp sang with colour and strength, injecting an important element of human warmth into the sound.

Gennadi Dubinsky, as Monterone the wronged father, was imposing though slightly less searing in delivering the plot’s actual curse in Act 1 than in his portentous and incisive power in shackles in Act 2.

Dalibor Jenis as Rigoletto and Gemma Nha as Gilda. Keith Saunders

The Opera Australia Chorus seemed to enjoy their depravity, and sang with an energised, full sound, the male members delivering conspiratorial whispered precision in the kidnap scene and a quite passable soft-shoe shuffle.

Under conductor Sesto Quatrini, the Opera Australia Orchestra highlighted the shudders and flashes of Verdi’s highly original orchestration with discreet balance. Simon Meadows (Marullo), Virgilio Marino (Borsa), Jonathan Alley (Ceprano), Dominica Matthews (a haughtily, ungracious Giovanna), Jennifer Black (Countess Ceprano) and Katherine Wiles (Page) adeptly filled out a cast in which nobody acts particularly admirably.

This production is a gem of Opera Australia’s repertoire though it doesn’t seek to problematise male possessiveness, which is the story’s real curse.

title: ASX loses ground on back of jobs data; Miners, James Hardie advance
text:
Advertisement

Updated BusinessMarketsWorld markets ASX loses ground on back of jobs data; Miners, James Hardie advance Staff writers Updated July 23, 2026 — 12:45pm ,first published July 23, 2026 — 5:18am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The Australian sharemarket has pared some of its strong early gains after the release of the nation’s latest job market figures kept the chances of an interest rate rise on the table. The S&P/ASX 200 was up 56 points, or 0.6 per cent, to 8879 as of 12.42pm AEST, led by the mining and financial sectors, which make up more than half of the local market. Wall Street swung between gains and losses throughout the session. Bloomberg The Australian Bureau of Statistics this morning reported that in June the jobless rate increased by 0.1 percentage points to 4.4 per cent. The under-employment rate also lifted, climbing to 6.5 per cent. The bureau said 76,300 jobs were created last month of which 47,000 were part-time. The ASX added 0.3 per cent on Wednesday. The Australian dollar was trading at US70.16¢.

Advertisement

In major corporate news, Macquarie Group said this morning its chief executive Shemara Wikramanayake is retiring as its CEO after eight years in the job, to be replaced by the head of its banking arm, Greg Ward. The investment banking giant said Wikramanayake, who is 64 and has been chief executive since late 2018, will retire from November this year. Ward, 58, is a Macquarie veteran who has run the group’s banking and financial services arm, which houses its rapidly-growing retail bank, since 2013. He joined Macquarie in 1996 and previously served as chief financial officer for 14 years. Related Article Opinion

Middle East at war The real oil shock from the Iran war might be just beginning Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist The shares were little moved on the news, up 0.5 per cent in early trade. Meanwhile, the big four banks all advanced, with CBA up 1.5 per cent, Westpac up 1.3 per cent, National Australia Bank up 1.6 per cent and ANZ Bank up 1.1 per cent. James Hardie jumped 6.7 per cent after the building materials maker - which makes most of its earnings in the US - said its June quarter sales beat its own forecasts, coming in at between $US1.45 billion ($2.1 billion) and $US1.48 billion, thanks to stronger-than-expected sales of its home sidings.

Advertisement

Mining giants BHP, Rio Tinto and Fortescue extended their strong run from Wednesday, up 1.4 per cent, 0.6 per cent and 1.7 per cent, respectively, in early afternoon trade. The gold miners also continued to rally as the precious metal extended its gains, supported by dip-buyers who scooped it up after prices had drifted lower in recent weeks on concerns that US inflationary pressure from higher oil prices will prompt the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates. Bullion rose as much as 2.1 per cent overnight to climb above $US4160 an ounce, after advancing 1.7 per cent the previous session. Gold miner Northern Star Resources was up 2.7 per cent, Evolution Mining gained 2.9 per cent and Newmont rose 1.5 per cent. Energy stocks had another strong morning as oil prices continued to climb, hitting a six-week high. Brent crude spiked in post-settlement trading overnight, rising near $US95 a barrel after Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels said they struck two Saudi tankers. Local oil and gas giant Woodside rose 0.5 per cent, and Santos added 1 per cent, even as it cut its production guidance for the second quarter due to challenges in the ramp-up of its Barossa and Pikka projects, and the timing of cargo receipts. Coal producers Yancoal and Whitehaven were up 1.4 and 2.6 per cent, respectively. Real estate investment trusts led early gains on the ASX, with warehouse and IT data centre owner Goodman Group up 1.9 per cent, and shopping centre landlords Scentre, Vicinity and Stockland up 2.1 per cent, 0.6 per cent and 0.7 per cent, respectively.

Advertisement

Tech stocks slumped, with software makers Xero and WiseTech falling 3.9 per cent and 4.5 per cent, while family tracking app Life 360 lost 5.1 per cent. The ASX’s early gains come even as Wall Street was drifting in mixed trading and oil prices climbed another 3 per cent to a six-week high as fighting continues in the war with Iran. The S&P 500 slipped 0.1 per cent, coming off its best day in three weeks, after swinging between modest losses and gains for much of the day. The Dow Jones closed flat and the Nasdaq composite lost 0.6 per cent. Tesla released its results after the closing bell, reporting negative free cash flow in the second quarter for the first time in more than two years as the Elon Musk-led EV maker accelerated spending on AI infrastructure, battery capacity, robotaxis and next-generation manufacturing. Tesla reported negative free cash flow of $US1.1 billion, compared with analysts’ expectation for cash burn of $US3.3 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG. EV sales in the quarter helped assuage some fears for now. Tesla delivered 480,126 vehicles in the second quarter, above Wall Street expectations and up from 384,122 vehicles a year earlier. Shares were down 3.3 per cent in extended trading. Alphabet topped Wall Street estimates for its second-quarter revenue, buoyed by continued strong demand for its cloud computing services from enterprises worldwide.

Advertisement

Revenue at Google Cloud rose 82 per cent to $US24.8 billion during the quarter ended June, accelerating from the 63 per cent jump reported in the preceding three months. Analysts on average expected a 64 per cent increase, according to data compiled by LSEG. Shares of the company were flat in extended trading. The majority of stocks in the S&P 500 rose after more companies reported strong profits for the spring. Expectations are high for the reports, and companies will need to match them after their stock prices already neared records on anticipation for them. Philip Morris International rose 3.3 per cent after the seller of Marlboro cigarettes reported stronger profit and revenue for the latest quarter than expected. Its shipments of smoke-free products rose 7.5 per cent. AT&T climbed 3.5 per cent after reporting a stronger profit than analysts expected. CEO John Stankey said the telecom is accelerating plans to send roughly $US10 billion to shareholders this year through share buybacks.

Advertisement

Super Micro Computer soared 20 per cent after the seller of AI servers said it expects to report stronger profit margins for the latest quarter than it had earlier forecast. It, though, also said that revenue will likely come in at the low end of its forecasted range . They helped offset an 8.7 per cent drop for GE Vernova, which reported a weaker profit for the latest quarter than analysts expected. Stocks broadly also felt pressure from continuing climbs for oil prices, which raise costs for most businesses and erode their profits. Related Article Opinion

Media & marketing It’s News Corp v the ‘byte burglars’: Murdochs fire up in AI content battle Elizabeth Knight Business columnist The price for a barrel of Brent crude oil, the international standard, rose 3.3 per cent to $US94 after briefly topping $US95 in the morning to touch its highest price in nearly six weeks. That’s up from less than $US72 early this month, which is roughly where it was before the war.

Advertisement

Rising oil prices are threatening a reacceleration of inflation. That in turn could push the Federal Reserve and other central banks to raise interest rates, which would slow economies and undercut prices for stocks and other investments. The yield on the 10-year Treasury edged up to 4.66 per cent from 4.63 per cent late Tuesday and from just 3.97 per cent before the war with Iran began. It’s already helped bring long-term US mortgage rates to their highest levels in nearly a year. Oil prices have climbed as fighting across the Middle East keeps oil tankers from using the Strait of Hormuz to exit the Persian Gulf. Normally, a fifth of all oil and natural gas traded passes through the narrow strait. In other international markets, indexes climbed in Europe following a mixed session in Asia. London’s FTSE 100 rose 1.2 per cent, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng fell 1 per cent for two of the world’s bigger moves. with AP, Reuters The Market Recap newsletter is a wrap of the day’s trading.
