title: Money to burn? Big Tech’s AI splurge is blowing out
text:
Advertisement

BusinessCompaniesAI Opinion Money to burn? Big Tech’s AI splurge is blowing out Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist July 23, 2026 — 12:01pm

July 23, 2026 — 12:01pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Amid a raft of impressive numbers in Alphabet’s second-quarter results, one stands out. While quadrupling its profits, Google’s parent company reported the first quarter of negative free cash flow in its history. The apparent massive lift in profit overstated Alphabet’s operating performance by including a $US98 billion ($140 billion) non-cash mark-up in the value of its investments in SpaceX and Anthropic, but the cash-burn in the quarter was very real. The cash burn is real: Alphabet has spent up big to make Google an AI giant. Getty It has become a common feature of big technology companies’ results as they plough ever-increasing amounts of cash and capital into artificial intelligence. In fact, both of those features of Alphabet’s numbers are common to the “hyperscalers” chasing their places in an AI-dominated future and share a common thread: the demands for capital to fund AI developments outstrip the ability of the companies to fund them internally.

Advertisement

Increasingly, even the biggest tech companies are calling on debt markets, raising equity from the sharemarket and investing in their rivals to create daisy chains of mutual dependence – and vulnerability. The demands aren’t diminishing. Alphabet had capital expenditures of $US44.9 billion in the quarter – more than double the amount invested the same quarter of last year, and about 25 per cent more than it spent in the first quarter. The falls in Alphabet and Tesla’s share prices after their results suggest that investors’ patience and tolerance for the ever-escalating costs of their AI plans are starting to wane. It had previously forecast capex of $US180 billion to $US190 billion for the full year, but has now raised that to $US195 billion to $US205 billion. Alphabet wasn’t the only big tech company reporting results where AI investing overshadowed the performance of its more established operations.

Advertisement

Elon Musk’s Tesla also released its June-quarter results this morning, which showed profits stayed essentially flat at $US1.1 billion, despite a 25 per cent jump in revenue. Tesla generates more than 70 per cent of its revenue from sales of its electric cars – it sold a lot more of them in the quarter, albeit with significantly lower margins – but it is trying to transition to an AI-facilitated future of Robotaxis and robots. Related Article AI OpenAI says its AI models went rogue and attacked a digital library It, too, experienced negative cash flows in the quarter as it ploughed $US5.8 billion into spending, much of it AI- and robotics-related. That was more that twice its $US2.5 billion of capex in the first quarter, but well short of the rate needed to meet the $US25 billion of investment Musk now says he is targeting for the full-year. For both Alphabet and Tesla there are clear signs of surging AI-related sales growth.

Advertisement

Alphabet’s cloud business revenues surged 82 per cent and its increasingly AI-integrated search unit also seems to be benefiting, while subscriptions to Tesla’s “Full-Self-Driving” automated driver-assist software, which costs $US99 a month, grew 15.6 per cent to 1.5 million. That’s a 56 per cent increase on their level a year ago. Both companies’ problem, and that of the entire AI sector, is that revenue growth rates are being outstripped by the growth rates in the spending required to keep up with the development of AI. Not only is the competition in AI increasingly being driven by the hardware – semiconductors, servers, data centres – but the intense competition, and the demand it is generating, is straining the capacity of the industry’s “picks and shovels” companies to deliver. The costs of “compute” are rising sharply and inexorably, and the timelines between committing the capital and generating revenue from it are starting to blow out. The falls in both companies’ share prices after their results (Alphabet was down 1.5 per cent and Tesla dropped 1.3 per cent) suggest that investors’ patience and tolerance for the ever-escalating costs of their AI plans are starting to wane.

Advertisement

Related Article AI Google is building an AI fence around the internet it once championed It’s not helping the companies or the environment in which they are seeking to raise capital that China seems to have a proliferation of open-source AI companies that can deliver near-comparable performance at a fraction of the cost of the hyperscalers, although the established tech giants do have the advantage of being able to integrate AI into their existing businesses and customer bases. Alphabet, like the other US hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, Oracle), used to be a “capital-light” business. Today, it and its peers are increasingly capital-intensive as the impact of the $US750 billion-plus they plan to spend this year – and the $US1 trillion-plus next year – continues to hit their balance sheets. Their financial profiles are being transformed, and their balance sheets are becoming leveraged. For instance, Alphabet’s total asset base has grown from $US595.3 billion a year ago to $US922 billion, with its long-term debt more than doubling from $US46.6 billion to $US98.2 billion to help fund that expansion. It also raised $US50 billion selling new shares as part of a planned $US80 billion capital raising.

Advertisement

At least the hyperscalers, and Tesla for that matter, have cashflows and earnings from their pre-existing businesses to help fund their AI investments. Start-ups like OpenAI and Anthropic don’t have those legacy cashflows and therefore are reliant on regular equity injections from investors (including their competitors) and, increasingly, bond issues. The sharemarket performance of SpaceX sounds a warning note, suggesting that not all things AI always rise in value. Getty Images There’s a circularity to the relationships between the industry participants that has so far enabled valuations to keep rising, which provides the necessary access to equity and debt markets (and paper profits from shareholdings in AI-driven companies). The sharemarket performance of another one of Musk’s companies, SpaceX, however, sounds a warning note suggesting that not all things AI always rise in value.

Advertisement

SpaceX debuted on the sharemarket last month at an initial public offering price of $US135 a share. The stock opened above $US150, spiked above $US200 within its first fortnight of trading – and is currently priced at about $US115. Related Article Opinion

Sharemarket Is SpaceX a meme stock? Elon Musk loses trillionaire status Kishor Napier-Raman Senior Business Writer The company has lost more than $US1 trillion of its peak market valuation and, with nearly another billion of “insider” shares coming out of lockup early next month, is at risk of falling further. SpaceX’s early market performance was an illustration of investors’ willingness to capitalise visions of what AI might be. Its subsequent fall signals that they also want some evidence that the vast amounts being invested in AI today will produce a return, commensurate with the risks, at some point in the not-too-distant future. title: Waterhouse-Bott win race for Leilani Lodge to spark major stable move
text:
The Australian Turf Club has cited safety concerns as a major factor in granting Leilani Lodge to Gai Waterhouse and Adrian Bott in a move that is set to signal the end of Tulloch Lodge, Sydney’s most successful stables.

The ATC announced on Thursday that Waterhouse and Bott would take over the lease of 65 boxes at the High Street stables established and made famous by Melbourne Cup king Bart Cummings from the early 1980s.

Trainer Gai Waterhouse with Just Fine at her Randwick stables in 2023 Kate Geraghty

Leilani Lodge was vacated early last year after Cummings’ son, Anthony, was stripped of his trainers’ licence and evicted. Ciaron Maher then beat Waterhouse and Bott to secure the lease, but the top Victorian trainer, a three-time Australian premiership winner, had not moved any horses in after months of significant refurbishments then a change of his plans to instead take over at Racing NSW-bought 125-box Crown Lodge at Warwick Farm.

Waterhouse and Bott this time edged out Michael Freedman on the short-list for Leilani Lodge, although he will be given an additional 20 boxes on course in a rejig of other stables.

title: Billion-dollar charity locked in $923m tax fight with the ATO
text:
Advertisement

Exclusive NationalPhilanthropy Billion-dollar charity locked in $923m tax fight with the ATO Patrick Begley July 23, 2026 — 11:47am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

His gardener and receptionist became millionaires overnight. His best friend and “right hand” man received $400 million. But the greatest beneficiary of Paul Ramsay’s will was his charity. Paul Ramsay died in 2014, leaving a fortune of more than $3.5 billion. Now, more than a decade after the private healthcare pioneer’s sudden death, the Paul Ramsay Foundation is locked in a $923 million fight with the Australian Taxation Office over income tax. Headquartered in a sandstone building with elaborate forged-steel gates in Sydney’s Darlinghurst, the foundation funds university research, Indigenous ventures and anti-poverty initiatives.

Advertisement

It also underpins the Ramsay Centre for Western Civilisation, a separate and at times contentious humanities program that champions a Western canon and values. The centre, whose board includes former prime minister Tony Abbott, received $37 million from the foundation in 2024-2025. The Darlinghurst headquarters of the Paul Ramsay Foundation. Louise Kennerley The tax fight, according to financial accounts submitted to the charities watchdog, stems from “legacy matters arising from the administration of the estate of the late Paul Ramsay”. Single, and with no children, Ramsay was worth about $3.5 billion when he succumbed to a suspected heart attack while sailing in Spain, aged 78. His fortune came from the private hospital empire he had built over decades, starting with an old guesthouse on Sydney’s north shore, which he converted into a psychiatric facility in 1964.

Advertisement

Ramsay Healthcare today trades publicly at a valuation of nearly $10 billion, operating across Australia, the United Kingdom and Europe. Related Article Education The $3.5 billion will, the instant millionaires ... and what was left out After the founder’s death, ownership of shares he owned in the company was transferred to a private ancillary fund – a vehicle often used by the wealthy to bankroll charitable ventures – as part of a $3 billion bequest to the foundation. The Ramsay fund is at the centre of the dispute with the tax office, which conducted an income tax audit for the years 2016, 2017 and 2018. “The total primary tax amount levied on the [fund] is $699 million,” the charity wrote in its annual accounts, which also referred to a $175 million penalty and $49 million in interest charges.

Advertisement

The fund objects to the income tax assessments. If unsuccessful, the Paul Ramsay Foundation will foot the bill. A foundation spokesman said the tax audit focused on steps taken in 2016 to administer Ramsay’s estate after he “died unexpectedly”. Related Article Investigation

Philanthropy ‘Next shiny thing’: Inside Twiggy Forrest’s $7 billion charity “The steps were taken with the benefit of tax advice from a leading accounting firm, as well as independent legal advice,” the spokesman said. He said the dispute did not relate to the foundation’s “charitable purpose or operations” and “does not impact the charity’s funding partners”. Those partners include the NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service, the University of Newcastle, RMIT, The Royal Women’s Hospital in Melbourne, the Queensland Performing Arts Trust, The Australia Institute and The Smith Family.

Advertisement

The foundation says it has distributed “more than $1.5 billion in funding to charities seeking to address disadvantage in Australia” since 2017. But the value of its assets has declined with falls in Ramsay Healthcare’s share price. A foundation subsidiary has sold shares in recent years to meet “cashflow needs”. A tax office spokesperson declined to comment, citing “obligations under taxpayer confidentiality laws”. title: I’d just gone through cancer. Then Blues bosses delivered a cruel blow
text:
The former Carlton captain opens up on cancer, chemotherapy, comebacks and the captaincy decision he didn’t see coming in an extract from his new book.

Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

It would have been pointless to treat round one 2022 like any other game, because it wasn’t. My cancer recurrence happened seven months prior, during which I had played this contest over and over in my head. I went for a walk that morning, listening to music during my stroll around the park, and found myself nearly crying because I was so close to the goal that had sustained me. I must have looked like an idiot. I didn’t know what to expect. The first half of my career, these clashes against the Tigers all went the same way. We were shit and they were very good, and so it would be a tight tussle until three-quarter-time, and in the nervous energy over what we thought was a chance at winning, we would falter and fold, or their class would prevail and they would win by four goals. It was almost a script. But this was a new era. New culture. New version. Hopefully a new story. I was just grateful to be playing. Grateful to even run out there. And then, in the second quarter, the ball came towards me near the centre circle, a stab pass into the middle, which I marked. I leaned off my line a little, feigning a handball, trying to sell enough candy to coax a twitching Shane Edwards off his mark. This was the first year of the controversial “stand” rule, and I’d already spotted something to exploit. If you could tease the man on the mark into shifting, even one step, you could take your 50-metre penalty and run. In the end, it wasn’t needed. Tom Lynch came from behind my left shoulder trying to get to Adam Saad, but the big man made the mistake of getting too close and coming through the protected area, in violation of another new rule tweak designed (and strictly enforced) to give the player with the ball maximum space. The umpire blew his whistle and I already knew I’d be having a shot. I tucked the Sherrin under my arm and trotted slowly inside our forward 50. Sam Docherty splits the sticks in that memorable first game of 2022. Getty Images As I set myself in front of the goal face, I had none of the big moment stuff you might expect in my head. I was not thinking this would be life-changing or iconic or somehow representative of anything. I simply followed my goalkicking routine, because that was me. Process and perform. My life could be chaos – illness, awards, reviews, pregnancy, pain – but I always had the ability to leave all that on the other side of the white line. Maybe that was the secret sauce in my career. I never got lost in the weeds on the field. And so, seven minutes and 53 seconds into the second quarter, I lined up and took 10 clean steps – I couldn’t have kicked it any better. The ball didn’t deviate as it split the sticks.

Advertisement

People still talk to me about that moment to this day, actually, stopping me in the street to share how it gave them chills, how it made them think of a loved one with cancer – someone who survived or who had died – and how something as simple as chipping a footy through the uprights could become a beacon of hope. Others tell me about how they were just going through a hard time in their lives and that goal shone a little light into their personal darkness. The game itself was tight until the third quarter, and then, instead of Richmond finding a way to pull clear, it was us who ran away with the four points. If I’ve made my path from chemotherapy to an AFL field seem seamless or smooth, then let me clarify: that’s not at all how it was. Chemo is essentially designed to take your immune system down to zero – it completely smashes you – but once it’s over, you get markedly better and better and better. Docherty at Carlton training in 2021, having undertaken chemotherapy for cancer treatment. AFL Photos I felt reasonable again pretty quickly, but I wasn’t ready for an elite training environment. Truth be told, I probably wasn’t even ready for that connection camp. I was properly crook, and I looked it too. I probably had a little stubble on my head, but that was all. I weighed maybe 74 kilograms, and I had to get back to around 85 before Jack Russell would even let me run. That meant low intensity exercise, starting with body weight squats and calf raises, trying to build my strength up to the point where I was healthy enough and strong enough that I could relieve the tension on my joints and handle the heavy work of rebuilding muscle mass. There were other challenges, even after I got back to full fitness and strength. One of the possible side effects of chemo is numbness or tingling in your extremities. I had it, so I played that entire year with the pads on the end of my fingers numb and tingly, as were the soles of my feet. All season long, I couldn’t quite feel the ball in my hands, beyond the weight of the thing. But the lack of touch in my soles was a bigger concern, because of my history of knee injuries.

Advertisement

In order to land properly and avoid injury when you’re running, you need something called proprioception. We all have it. It’s the sense that allows you to know where your limbs are in space. Sensing where your feet are at any given moment, and therefore where and how they will land on the ground, is one of the most important tools for preventing ACL injuries. Having a lack of sensation in the bottom of your feet is pretty far from ideal. I had minor calf strains all year, but I played through them. I did a calf halfway through the pre-season, in fact, which threatened to derail my return match. I just didn’t let it. I was focused on making it through everything and into finals. It honestly seemed like every game meant more that year – like I couldn’t take anything for granted – so there was no time for a rest. At least that was the way I saw it; the club, it turned out, had other ideas. In January, before the season began, I got a tap on the shoulder for a meeting with our new CEO, Brian Cook, who had been there all of three weeks. I’d never spoken with him before. Vossy (coach Michael Voss) was there too, who had been at Carlton for just a couple of months. And Brad Lloyd, our long-time general manager of the football department. They sat me down and shared their common thought: “For your health and wellbeing, we think it’s best that you’re not captain of the club or part of the leadership group this year.” Co-captains Sam Docherty and Patrick Cripps lead their team out on to the field in 2021. Getty Images I was stunned. I hadn’t expected this conversation, but I computed the give up the captaincy, allowing Crippa (Patrick Cripps) to lead solo, flanked by a few fresh faces like Charlie Curnow and Jacob Weitering and Sam Walsh. New voices for a new era. But the bit that really hit me – and really hurt me, if I’m honest – was being asked to leave the leadership group. I’d just gone through the hardest thing I’ve ever experienced in my life, and I was already coping with great uncertainty. Taking me out of the players’ leadership group – where I could be part of the conversation about what we were doing and why, and help set the direction for the boys – seemed almost cruel. They believed they were doing me a favour – they argued that I would still be viewed as a leader but wouldn’t have to bear the attached responsibility – but honestly it felt like something that gave me purpose and joy was being taken away.

Advertisement

Perhaps naively, I had thought that if something like that were going to happen, there’d at least be a conversation about it: Hey, this is what we think, tell us what you think and let’s come to a mutual decision about what’s best for everyone. I actually went back in for another meeting the next day to plead my case or have that negotiation, but their plan was set in stone. I couldn’t wrap my head around the fact that two people I hardly knew were making a decision about my life, and claiming it was the best thing for me, without knowing anything about who I was, alongside one person who did know me well and therefore should have known better. Every time I punched the info into my head it felt like an error. Yes, I’d been ill – but I was also 28. I wasn’t some veteran in decline. I’d averaged 24 touches a game in 2021 before my syndesmosis tore under a pair of Crows. I had so much to give. It was impossible not to roll negative thoughts around in my head. Do they not want me in there because I’m the guy who speaks his mind? Is this part of a broader plan to reduce my role in the side? Has my illness stamped my papers as a liability? I learnt to live with my new reality, in which calendar invitations to certain meetings stopped appearing, and I was on the outside of something I used to help drive. All I could do was concentrate on the things I could control. That will be familiar to anyone whose identity has taken a blow, who’s been jolted out of a familiar rhythm, who’s lost their job or their sense of self. My solution was to focus on my own performance and continue to lead with my actions. Personally, I felt like I was on fire. By almost any on-field metric, it was my best-performed season. I was flying again. I felt incredibly proud of it, too, taking a perverse joy in managing my discomfort to achieve difficult things. When the season was over, I finished second to Crippa in the B&F – and this was the year he won his first Brownlow Medal. You might remember it for the two-match suspension he was given in round 21 for a high hit on Callum Ah Chee. Carlton engaged noted Queen’s counsel Chris Townsend to lead an appeal, arguing that Crippa had been denied “natural justice” in his initial hearing.

Advertisement

title: Billionaires’ factory? Macquarie chief to walk away with $880m in shares
text:
Advertisement

Macquarie Group chief executive Shemara Wikramanayake is retiring as boss of the investment giant after eight years in the job, to be replaced by the head of its banking and financial services arm, Greg Ward. The investment bank on Thursday said Wikramanayake, who is 64 and has been chief executive since late 2018, will retire from November this year. Shemara Wikramanayake has been in charge of Macquarie since 2018. Leigh Vogel Wikramanayake will walk from the top job with shares currently worth more than $880 million after close to 40 years with the financial giant. According to her most recent director’s shareholding notice filed with the ASX, Wikramanayake held more than 3.47 million Macquarie shares which closed at $254.93 on Wednesday.

Advertisement

Wikramanayake also retains another 440,000 performance rights over shares that may vest in the future depending on the group’s performance. Related Article Executive pay Half of the ASX’s highest-paid CEOs lead US-based companies Wikramanayake said on Wednesday she was leaving at the right time for both her and Macquarie. “I’ve always said I’m here as long as Macquarie needs me, and I think the business is in great [shape] now, and I think the board has chosen a great next CEO in Greg, who I’ve worked with for 30 years, not just in the awesome work he’s been doing with the team in BFS (banking and financial services), but also as CFO through things like the GFC,” she said. Sydney-based Macquarie is colloquially known as the “millionaires’ factory” for its high pay packets and culture that rewards financial performance.

Advertisement

Last year Wikramanayake was awarded $26.5 million in pay, less than the $35.4 million paid to the executive in charge of its booming commodities business, Simon Wright. Ward was paid $13 million last year. Under Wikramanayake’s leadership Macquarie has expanded substantially in domestic banking but remains a global business at heart, earning about two-thirds of its revenue overseas. Greg Ward is currently the head of banking and financial services group at Macquarie. Kate Geraghty Its market capitalisation has risen from about $39 billion when Wikramanayake started in the job to about $98 billion. Macquarie chairman Glenn Stevens also dropped a potential bombshell on the KPMG scandal, telling shareholders the group is reviewing the embattled firm’s capability to deliver on the lucrative Macquarie audit tender it won last year due to the exodus of senior staff over the scandal.

Advertisement

Macquarie has also instigated an external review of KPMG’s pursuit of the group’s audit contract which was worth $69 million last year. Related Article Exclusive

Consulting KPMG whistleblower refused to move to Australia, fearing alleged bullying “The board has made formal inquiries of KPMG, and they include regarding KPMG’s ongoing capability and capacity to deliver the audit, given that some people have left that firm, and an assessment of the integrity of KPMG’s pursuit of our audit tender process,” Stevens said. “That’s to be supported by an external review conducted by Allens, with a scope that has been agreed by Macquarie management. We’ll keep shareholders informed as this matter unfolds over the period ahead.” Allens is the same law firm which is currently investigating - on behalf of KPMG - the whistleblower claims that senior audit partners illicitly accessed client information to win new customers.

Advertisement

The Macquarie contract was one of the tenders mentioned in the allegations. Stevens continued to defend the integrity of Macquarie’s tender process and the role of former KPMG partner Michelle Hinchliffe, who heads Macquarie’s audit committee. “We remain confident that the process that we ran was a robust one, and that we found the right balance between using Michelle’s highly developed skills, which are very beneficial for the company and very beneficial in ensuring we have a good tender, and managing her potential conflicts resulting from her previous employment, which were all fully disclosed and managed.” Macquarie shares rose 0.5 per cent on Thursday morning as the bank also provided a first-quarter update, saying trading conditions had been “satisfactory”.

Advertisement

Citi analyst Thomas Strong said Ward was a logical choice for Macquarie, saying he was well known and regarded in the market and the banking and financial services division had been a strong success story within Macquarie. “Overall, given the internal appointment we expect a smooth transition,” Strong said. Macquarie chairman Glenn Stevens told shareholders the group is reviewing KPMG’s capability to deliver on the lucrative Macquarie audit tender it won last year. Ben Searcy Stevens said: “The board extends its heartfelt thanks to Shemara, who leaves Macquarie in a strong position, with sustained growth across each of our diverse businesses and a highly capable senior team.” “Over her last eight years as CEO, and for almost four decades with the company, Shemara has steered Macquarie through expansion into new markets, the dislocation of the COVID pandemic, and significantly enhanced recognition of our brand and the value we bring to global clients and communities.

Advertisement

“In considering the transition from Shemara’s groundbreaking tenure, the board is pleased to have an experienced candidate of Greg’s calibre to lead Macquarie through its next phase of growth and looks forward to working with him as he brings his global experience and proven ability as a business and technology leader to build on this momentum in the years ahead,” Stevens said. Related Article Updated

Executive pay The $35 million Macquarie man who out-earned his own CEO Ward, 58, is a Macquarie veteran who has run the banking and financial services arm of Macquarie, which houses its rapidly growing retail bank, since 2013. He joined Macquarie in 1996 and previously served as chief financial officer for 14 years. Under Ward’s leadership, the retail bank has aggressively taken on the big four in home loans and deposits, investing heavily in its technology platform and expanding to become the fifth-biggest bank in the country. title: From a loved old clunker to driverless cars: What are we losing on this road trip?
text:
Advertisement

Tony Wright’s Column NationalDriverless cars From a loved old clunker to driverless cars: What are we losing on this road trip? Tony Wright July 23, 2026 — 11:40am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

At a time when regulators are assessing when to let driverless cars loose on our streets, when one in four new cars sold in Australia is electric and just about the last internal combustion conveyances are family SUVs or giant utes, I still get a kick out of remembering the day I bought my first car. It was an old Holden built in the wrong year with the wrong motor to be properly cool, and I didn’t exactly buy it; I talked my dad into handing over the cash. The joy of owning a classic old Holden. Artwork: Matt Davidson I’d just finished school and holiday jobs were never going to pay for even this 1963 Holden EJ with so many miles on the clock I avoided looking at the number. My father tried to steer me towards an Austin, a Vauxhall or a Volkswagen.

Advertisement

Nothing doing. They were all impossibly daggy. And there it was. A car had its own personality – its own credibility – transferred to its owner, starting with the brand. A Holden was Australian, and it wasn’t a Ford. Young Australian men those days identified themselves as Holden blokes or Ford boys. It was as important as choosing a footy team. I felt as if I were floating six inches above the vinyl bench seat as I drove out of the car yard. The road was mine.

Advertisement

Why, life was mine! I could choose any destination I wished. It was simply a matter of mashing the clutch, choosing a gear (the gearstick stuck out of the steering column, an arrangement known as “three on the tree”) and pointing the front wheels in the right direction. Here was independence. Autonomy with a steering wheel and an accelerator. Freedom. Holden EJ, a little worse for wear, but to the author’s teenage eyes, such a machine spelled freedom. The old motor chewed oil and the radiator was given to boiling on long uphill stretches, but it took me to dances in towns all over the place, to the drive-in, to surf beaches and late-night hamburger joints.

Advertisement

When I got a job as a cadet journalist at the local paper, my weekly pay was $26.50. It stretched OK: petrol was 33 cents a gallon, or nine cents a litre. A six-pack of beers was about $1.40, a hamburger with the lot was 30 cents and a pack of cigarettes was 40 cents. I lived rent-free at my parents’ house, and it came with my mother’s cooking. Editor's pick Explainer

Real life What’s the secret to a well-rounded life? These researchers have a theory Life, in short, rolled out like a smooth road waiting to be driven, and I drove lots of them, though most were rough. Soon, in hopeless pursuit of more cool, there came a Mini Cooper, an Austin-Healey Sprite – both driven with too much enthusiasm and not much care by previous owners – and later Fiat 124 sports machines, which spent a lot of expensive time sitting in workshops. Motorbikes came along and I was captured by wind in the face, a satisfying burble following everywhere and an even greater sense of freedom. The best machine of all was also the worst from any sensible perspective.

Advertisement

It was a little box on tiny wheels. A canvas roof theoretically kept out some of the rain and the wind of winter, and was removed to ensure you were burned by the sun all summer. The brakes were close to non-existent until one at the front grabbed hard and spun the vehicle in a perfect 360-degree tyre-shredding loop. It was dangerous, but the biggest danger was dying from smiling with pleasure. It was a Mini-Moke. A Mini-Moke. A magnificently dangerous smile-inducing machine. Instagram @woogsworld One freezing winter’s day I drove the Moke up the Hume Highway from Albury to Canberra. I tucked in behind a big tourist coach in the futile hope I’d get a bit of heat from its exhaust and some protection from the needles of slicing rain. It was white-knuckle driving, until the knuckles turned blue.

Advertisement

But oh, the sense of achievement when I arrived alive at my destination! I’d done something that set every nerve teetering at its farthest edge. For unrelenting hours. Ancient history, of course. Lots of young people, for many good reasons (Uber, for one), are not excited by cars and don’t bother getting a driver’s licence these days. The idea of using a stick shift to change gears disappeared shortly after the dinosaurs died out. Cars no longer have personalities, whatever efforts advertising agencies take to persuade us that they do. Designers, constrained by the need to make large passenger vehicles slip through the air with maximum efficiency, have settled on a universal style. It has become hard to tell one SUV or sedan from another.

Advertisement

You don’t so much drive newer cars as sit in them and let squadrons of sensors and cameras and radar do the work. Electric vehicles have become computers on wheels. It means safety has taken giant leaps. Comfort, too. Next will be driverless cars. Driverless robotaxis are already navigating the city streets of the United States and China. In an age of artificial intelligence, it fits. Cartoonist Cathy Wilcox’s view of a driverless car. Cathy Wilcox

Advertisement

We no longer need to strain our brains or leaf through books in search of the combined knowledge of humanity. It’s in the Cloud, accessible with a few prompts on a keyboard. We no longer need to listen to a motor’s revs to gauge when to change gears, to avoid skidding sideways on a bend or even be prepared every second of a journey to hit the brakes. Those hundreds of sensors take care of all that. Related Article Good Weekend This is the car driver’s version of the Sydney v Melbourne debate Sometimes, though, the happy memory of slugging through the gears on an old Holden or a Moke in search of freedom leaves me wondering how much personal autonomy and how much raw sensory experience we should surrender to the easy passivity of artificial intelligence – which is seamlessly pilfering human jobs as we speak – and its wheeled metaphor, the driverless vehicle. It’s over, of course. As over as it was for the Luddites desperately smashing the machines of an industrial revolution stealing their livelihoods, crafts and skills.

Advertisement

Best, perhaps, to hold our memories close and polish them every now and then. Evolution doesn’t wait. Get the day’s breaking news, entertainment ideas and a long read to enjoy. Sign up to receive our Evening Edition newsletter.

title: There’s one major flaw with new ‘business class’ day trip to a world wonder
text:
Advertisement

InspirationNew Zealand There’s one major flaw with new ‘business class’ day trip to a world wonder Natasha Bazika Updated July 23, 2026 — 11:39am ,first published July 23, 2026 — 5:00am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Anyone who has ever flown business class knows the real luxury isn’t the wider seat or the gourmet meals (though they’re definitely perks). It’s the curtain, a visual barrier that separates you from the frantic, elbow-to-elbow situation of the main cabin. RealNZ and Altitude Tours have essentially pulled that velvet curtain across the day trip from Queenstown to Milford Sound with their new premium tour, Milford Sound Business Class. Milford Sound on the MV Sinbad. The upgrade starts at 7.30am. Instead of the typical 50-seat coach, I’m in a 14-seat Mercedes Sprinter. A lambswool blanket is left on my seat and a ginger tonic waits in the pocket, a preemptive strike against the notoriously winding roads ahead. Our driver, Ash, handles the two-hour stretch to Te Anau with a running commentary of fast facts. My favourite is the Maori legend of Lake Wakatipu, New Zealand’s longest lake, said to have been formed by the burning body of a giant taniwha named Matau. “Some say you can still see his heart beating with the rise and fall of the lake level,” she says over the microphone. “It pulses around 12 centimetres every half hour.”

Advertisement

By our morning tea stop in Te Anau, I’m entirely sold on the road experience. But the weight of the $NZ699 ($575) a person ticket rests on the cruise and the five-course tasting menu. To deliver it, they’ve partnered with chef Tom Hishon from Auckland’s acclaimed seafood restaurant, Kingi, to bring the sensibility of a high-end urban restaurant and set it afloat in the middle of Fiordland. Sign up for the Traveller Deals newsletter Get exclusive travel deals delivered straight to your inbox. Sign up now. Scene-stealer – a Milford Sound waterfall. After a few scenic stops, we arrive at the Milford Sound Cruise Terminal. At the docks, a handful of boats loom, all much larger and more crowded than our vessel, MV Sinbad. Despite the vaguely villainous name, it’s a good-looking boat, white-hulled with a navy top. As we step on board, a glass of Cloudy Bay bubbles is pressed into my hand, and I clock the first course already waiting, a seeded sourdough bun with whipped kelp butter and a rich bowl of wapiti (elk) bone broth. Inside, curved banquette seating runs along the windows, with carved Maori detailing and sculptural works along the walls. The boat pulls away from the dock, and I see the problem straight away. On a plane, you sit still and accept what’s served. Here, the experience is constantly split between food and landscape.

Advertisement

I sit down for a course, spot a waterfall spilling down the cliffs, and run to the top deck. I come down for the next dish, paua served in-shell with black garlic, only for the captain to steer us beneath Stirling Falls moments later for what she calls a “glacial facial”. Another few bites arrive, Mount Cook Alpine caviar served on a potato rosti with sour cream and a Titi (muttonbird) croquette with a delightful citrus spin. The menu is by chef Tom Hishon of the acclaimed Auckland restaurant Kingi. For the main, we scoop up Fiordland wapiti (elk) tartare with potato crisps, and swallow in one bite smoked eel under a slice of beetroot. The windows at each table frame the landscape, but on this unusually bright day, they mostly reflect glare and streaks of salt. Besides, no one comes to Milford Sound to stare at it through a window. Someone shouts “dolphins!” at the bow, and every passenger clears out. No one comes back in a hurry, prompting the staff to bring dessert up instead, a flat white-inspired dish served in a glass.

Advertisement

The food is well executed, exactly what you would expect from Hishon, but the portions are too light for a 12-hour day. That becomes most obvious during the optional wine pairing, where generous pours arrive, two at a time, alongside one-bite courses. Related Article Opinion

Adventure holidays I went on my first big hike in my 40s. It changed the way I see travel Bianca O'Neill Contributor Drinking like that on an empty stomach before a three-hour drive back to Queenstown is a tough sell. For a faster return, you can skip the road and fly back by plane ($NZ500) or helicopter ($NZ1000), but that doesn’t fix the issue. There just isn’t quite enough food for the day. A more substantial main would have made a real difference here. Those of us who skip the scenic flight back load onto the van and find a packet of chips and a chocolate bar waiting on our seats. The crinkle of packets being opened and finished fills the van before we’ve even passed the edge of the national park. THE DETAILS

Advertisement

CRUISE

RealNZ Milford Sound Business Class starts from $NZ699 a person and includes return transfers from selected Queenstown accommodation, morning tea and coffee in Te Anau, a five-course tasting menu, and a two-hour Milford Sound cruise. Guests can upgrade to a scenic fixed-wing flight back from around $NZ500, or a helicopter flight with optional alpine landing from around $NZ1000, weather permitting. The optional wine pairing is $NZ65 a person, payable on the day. See realnz.com STAY

QT Queenstown (qthotels.com) has rooms from around $NZ450 a night. In Te Anau, Tasman Holiday Parks (tasmanholidayparks.com) has self-contained cabins, apartments and powered sites; one-bedroom apartments from around $NZ275 a night. See tasmanholidayparks.com; qthotels.com/queenstown FLY

Air New Zealand, Qantas and Virgin Australia operate direct flights from Brisbane, Sydney and Melbourne to Queenstown. HIRE CAR

Snap Rentals has a branch five minutes from Queenstown Airport with a free shuttle service. It’s also one of the few rental companies offering one-way hires, so you don’t need to return the car to Queenstown if you plan to drive elsewhere. See snaprentals.co.nz The writer travelled as a guest of Luxury New Zealand Collection. See luxurynewzealandcollection.com

The Olympics countdown clock unveiled in Queen Street Mall in Brisbane. Brisbane Times/William Davis

title: Races such as The Everest deserved elevation. But has it gone too far?
text:
Advertisement

SportRacingHorse racing Opinion Races such as The Everest deserved elevation. But has it gone too far? Darren Kane Sports Columnist July 23, 2026 — 11:30am

July 23, 2026 — 11:30am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

In a little over a month, spring will spring and many a person’s fancy will turn to the sounds of hooves thumping on turf. Three years ago, I opined that it was contrived nonsense that The Everest wasn’t by then a group 1 race. On pure numbers, under the rules as written, no cogent argument could be maintained against its elevation. Ka Ying Rising cleaned up in the 2025 edition of The Everest. Getty Images I also proffered that Racing Australia was manifestly derelict in not elevating the race years earlier, and questions needed to be asked. The Everest is now a group 1 race, and separately, questions were asked. Just not by anyone in Australia. Last December, the Asian Racing Federation exhausted its patience. Its Asian Pattern Committee seized control of decisions on the status of every black type race in Australia. Every group 1, 2, 3 and Listed race.

Advertisement

Its reasoning was pointed, in that Australia hasn’t operated a properly functioning quality control system for its elite races in a decade. The national pattern committee last met in 2018. Since then, crickets. Classifying Australia’s best races, the currency on which a multibillion-dollar breeding industry trades, is now determined by a committee whose members are drawn from the racing authorities of Hong Kong, Japan, New Zealand, South Africa and the UAE. Odd, much? Everest day has quickly become a highlight of the Australian racing calendar. Dion Georgopoulos The ARF dangled the threat of demoting Australia from the highest standard of the global directory of stakes races – alongside Britain, Ireland, France, Japan and the USA. How did we arrive at this nadir?

Advertisement

Interstate jealousy, chiefly. Racing Australia’s constituent bodies wasted years trying to agree on national black-type guidelines. Racing NSW, understandably impatient, started “upgrading” its own races such as the Golden Gift and The Warra from October 2024. James McDonald and Chris Waller lift the Golden Eagle trophy after Autumn Glow’s 2025 success. Getty Images They were upgrades the rest of the world refused to recognise, thus never filtering into international sales catalogues. There was approximately 18 races, run under gradings that existed mostly in the minds of their promoters. It was an untenable impasse. The APC has delivered its first tranche of classification decisions. Now, 30 Australian races have been upgraded or admitted to stakes status, many of them only battle-worn punters will have even heard of. None were downgraded. Seventy-five per cent of those races are run in NSW.

Advertisement

The $10 million Golden Eagle vaults from carrying no black type status to the highest echelon, becoming Australia’s 77th top-tier race for the upcoming season. In context, there were roughly 64 group 1s in Australia a quarter-century ago. The Victoria Derby, The Metropolitan and Perth’s Railway Stakes, each examined for possible demotion, survive intact. Just imagine if the Victoria Derby was downgraded. ‘Here’s the rub: group 1 status is meant to denote “The best of the best”.’ To be clear, the argument I prosecuted for The Everest in 2023 was that the ARF’s Ground Rules are objective. If a race’s ratings clear the prescribed benchmark across its recent renewals, elevation should be axiomatic. The APC’s rules ordinarily require a race to enter the pattern at group 3 and ascend; but exceptional circumstances provisions exist, used previously for The Everest and Melbourne’s All-Star Mile, permitting direct positioning at the summit. The Golden Eagle’s ratings justify the use of the exception.

Advertisement

Rules honestly applied are the point here, and on that measure there can’t be a complaint. But here’s the rub: group 1 status is meant to denote “The best of the best”. Mark Zahra and Observer win the 2025 Victoria Derby at Flemington. Getty Images Scarcity isn’t a by-product of the classification, as opposed to it being the essential point of the exercise. Australia will now conduct 77 group 1 races per season. Great Britain and Ireland stage about 50 combined. Japan runs roughly two dozen. Hong Kong, whose representatives sit on the committee now grading our races, conducts a dozen. Even the United States, with a human population 12 times Australia’s, hosts about 110 group 1s annually.

Advertisement

Australia races more horses, at more meetings, on more racecourses than almost anywhere. A bigger racing population can sustain a bigger elite, but the trend is concerning. No Australian race has been downgraded since August 2012. If Treasurer Jim Chalmers ever plunged his fingers into horse racing once he’s done with the rest of the economy, he’d call that inflationary. Inflation works on race classifications the same as it does on money, where every unwarranted note printed devalues the genuine article. If everything becomes elite, nothing is. What’d happen if the FIFA World Cup were an annual thing; or if Rory McIlroy were asked to defend his Green Jacket once a semester, even when Augusta’s flowers aren’t blooming? It’s the owners of horses that win authentic group 1 races, and the breeders who sell their progeny on the strength of those two words appearing on a catalogue page, who quietly wear the cost.

Advertisement

The next problem is geographic. Twenty-two of 30 elevated races are in NSW. What Racing NSW and its senior management have done for the business of thoroughbred racing over the last 20 years, traced back to prosecuting the race fields case to the High Court 20 years ago, is magnificent and extraordinary. Kerrin McEvoy and Redzel claimed the first edition of The Everest in 2017. Getty Images But prizemoney has become a weapon. Money buys fields, and fields generate ratings, and ratings satisfy ground rules. On the bare numbers, the Sydney races arguably deserve their new status. Racing elsewhere, though, is not thriving the same way; in some places it’s withering.

Advertisement

Related Article Opinion

Concussion crisis CTE is a frightening thing. But banning risky sports isn’t the answer Darren Kane Sports Columnist Now chase that logic to its terminus. If black type simply tracks prizemoney, the pattern migrates permanently to wherever the money is on offer: Sydney first, Melbourne second and daylight third. The signature races of South Australia, Western Australia and Queensland drift towards oblivion while Randwick’s five-year-old inventions leapfrog everything. A national pattern concentrated in two cities isn’t a national pattern. It’s a duopoly, with regional exhibition matches tacked on. In fairness, the APC is no rubber stamp. In September 2025, it rejected a Racing Australia application to upgrade more than 40 races, precisely because not a single downgrade was offered in exchange.

Advertisement

This month, several races were admitted at levels below what Australia requested. Is all of this good for racing? The evidence is incomplete. The APC meets in September to determine the remainder of the calendar, including, presumably, the downgrades. If the Sydney Cup, the Adelaide Cup and their kind are assessed without sentiment and demoted where the numbers require, Australia will possess a credible, functioning, two-directional pattern. That will be painful but necessary. If instead September passes and the ladder still points skywards, then what happened this month wasn’t quality control, as distinct from being a coronation of the status quo, with the dilution of Australian black type handed to an international imprimatur. In 2023, I wrote that The Everest being padlocked in the freezer was wrong. The new question is harder. Not whether one race deserves elevation, but whether a grade shared by 77 races can still claim to identify the best of the best. Racing’s administrators, wherever they now sit, ought to remember it.

title: The Rinehart effect, world record watch and a bid for history: What to know before the Aussies hit the pool in Glasgow
text:
Advertisement

SportSwimmingCommonwealth Games The Rinehart effect, world record watch and a bid for history: What to know before the Aussies hit the pool in Glasgow Tom Decent July 23, 2026 — 11:30am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Haven’t watched any swimming since the 2024 Paris Olympics? Or maybe you only caught a glimpse of the Australian trials in Sydney last month? Here’s your quick guide to the pool at the upcoming Commonwealth Games in Glasgow. Australia’s men’s and women’s 4x100m freestyle relay teams celebrate gold medals at last year’s world championships in Singapore. Getty Images What is the schedule? Competition begins on Friday in Glasgow and runs for six days. Heats are in the morning at 10.30am local time (7.30pm AEST), with finals running from 7pm (4am AEST). Able-body and para swimmers are both competing, with 56 gold medals up for grabs at the Tollcross International Swimming Centre, which is a small swimming facility. Australia is almost certain to top the medal tally and will win a high percentage of medals.

Advertisement

Which Aussies are a real chance of winning gold? Freestyle is far and away Australia’s greatest strength. On the women’s side, Meg Harris (50m freestyle), Mollie O’Callaghan (100m and 200m freestyle) and Lani Pallister (400m, 800m and 1500m freestyle) start as favourites in their individual events. Among the men, Cam McEvoy (50m freestyle), Kyle Chalmers (100m freestyle) and Sam Short (400m freestyle) headline Australia’s best chances. Meg Harris wins gold in the women’s 50m freestyle at the 2025 world swimming championships. Getty Images Short will also contest the 800m and 1500m freestyle and is a big gold medal chance in both, although Ireland’s Daniel Wiffen looms as his biggest threat if he’s anywhere near his best. Short was arguably the most impressive swimmer at Australia’s trials and is capable of sweeping all three events. He’ll also race the 200m freestyle. Zac Stubblety-Cook shapes as the man to beat in the 200m breaststroke, while Sam Williamson is firmly in the hunt in the 50m breaststroke and potentially the 100m breaststroke.

Advertisement

Jenna Forrester (200m and 400m individual medley), Lizzy Dekkers (200m butterfly) and Alex Perkins (100m butterfly) also enter as genuine gold medal contenders, with the absence of the United States, China and Canadian superstar Summer McIntosh leaving several events significantly more open. There are eight relays and Australia could win all of them. Four years ago, they took out seven of the relay golds on offer, with the men’s 4x100m medley relay the only one to fall short as England took the gold. Can Mollie O’Callaghan make history? Emma McKeon dominated the Birmingham Commonwealth Games, but O’Callaghan still left with five gold and two silver. This time, she could produce an even bigger haul.

Advertisement

Six, seven or eight gold medals are within reach, with relays giving O’Callaghan plenty of chances to win medals. The Australian record is six gold medals at a single Commonwealth Games, shared by Susie O’Neill (1998), Ian Thorpe (2002) and McKeon in Birmingham. Mollie O’Callaghan was the standout for Australia at the 2025 world swimming championships. Getty Images However, backstroke is the intriguing wildcard. With Kaylee McKeown absent due to glandular fever, O’Callaghan could fancy her chances in the 50m backstroke. She has not entered the 100m backstroke. O’Callaghan is no stranger to huge programs, but with the Pan Pacific Championships only about three weeks later in California, she may decide against overloading herself.

Advertisement

Adam Peaty watch The biggest international name in the pool is arguably Great Britain’s Adam Peaty, although at the Commonwealth Games he competes for England. Unlike the Olympics, where British athletes represent Team GB, swimmers from the United Kingdom compete for their individual home nations – England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. The six-time Olympic medallist won 100m breaststroke gold in Rio (2016) and Tokyo (2021) before claiming silver in Paris. Still the world record holder in the 100m breaststroke, Peaty has spent the past two years battling injuries and time away from the sport. Never write off a champion, but his showdown with Williamson looms as a beauty. Who benefits from the absence of McKeown and McIntosh? Two of the biggest stars in world swimming won’t be in Glasgow, with no McKeown and McIntosh in action.

Advertisement

Between them, they could easily have claimed eight individual gold medals, leaving several events suddenly wide open. Someone like Forrester or Dekkers, both world championships medallists, could step up. Canada’s Kylie Masse is also a big shot now in the 50m and 100m backstroke with McKeown out but Australia’s Iona Anderson could easily come home with a couple of gold medals in those events. Sam Short world record watch The 22-year-old is obsessed with going under three minutes, 40 seconds and breaking Lukas Martens’ 400m freestyle world record of 3:39.96. Short’s times at the major meets over the past three years have been solid and he clocked 3:40.67 at trials last month.

Advertisement

Coach Damien Jones believes Short is in the best shape of his career and another swimmer pushing him through the first 300 metres could be exactly what’s needed. The key will be attacking the race early and hanging on. Cam McEvoy’s last hurrah? The world record holder did exactly what he needed to at the Australian trials, clocking 21.32 in the 50m freestyle to comfortably qualify. Now the focus shifts to chasing an even faster time. Australian swimmer Cam McEvoy is the world record holder in the men’s 50m freestyle. Audrey Richardson The signs are encouraging. McEvoy has already swum unofficial 25m freestyle world record times – performances that won’t count because they aren’t ratified by World Aquatics. He has also recently done a 35m metre personal best at training.

Advertisement

Expect McEvoy to cruise through the heats and semi-finals before emptying the tank in the one-lap final where it’s his race to lose. Can Australia win every freestyle event? Every freestyle title looks attainable but the hardest of the lot will be the men’s 200m freestyle. Great Britain has dominated the event recently and, despite competing as separate home nations at the Commonwealth Games, still boasts remarkable depth. Matt Richards owns the fastest time in the world this year at 1:44.77 while Australians Short (1:45.16) and Kai Taylor (1:45.30) also impressed at trials.

Advertisement

The 200m freestyle is one of swimming’s most tactical races, where the fastest heat time does not always guarantee victory. Related Article Exclusive

Commonwealth Games After four years, this swim star is ready to speak about ‘the worst thing that ever happened to me’ “When you look at the times on paper, we’re in a pretty strong position to go pretty well on the medal tally,” Short said. “The 200 freestyle will be a bit tough. The British team are the twice defending Olympic champs in the 4x200 [freestyle relay] and the depth they have in that is pretty crazy.” The Gina effect

Advertisement

Australia’s swimmers already have plenty of motivation, but there is another incentive this year. Mining billionaire Gina Rinehart will again reward medal-winning Dolphins with bonuses of $20,000 for gold, $15,000 for silver and $10,000 for bronze. The payments could tempt some swimmers to contest busier programs than usual.

title: The superannuation 'blind spot' funding Australian gambling companies
text:
In brief Advocates say Australians may not be aware their super fund is investing billions into the gambling industry.

Some super funds do not apply investment screens to gambling companies.

Australia's 20 biggest superannuation funds have almost $15 billion invested in gambling-related companies, despite growing concern about the social harm caused by the industry.

A report released on Thursday by the Alliance for Gambling Reform found the sector holds $14.8 billion in gambling stocks.

Australia's largest fund, Australian Super, has the biggest exposure at $4.9 billion — more than double any other fund — while Australian Retirement Trust, Colonial First State, UniSuper and Aware Super round out the top five.

Gambling company Aristocrat Leisure is just one of the companies into which retirement funds are flowing.

Despite many funds having responsible investment policies, approaches to gambling-related risks remain "limited, inconsistent and largely inadequate", according to chief executive of the Alliance for Gambling Reform, Martin Thomas.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

He said many Australians would be shocked to learn how much of their money was being invested into the industry.

"There's starting to be a realisation just of how socially damaging gambling is," he told the Australian Associated Press.

"We see bankruptcies, mental health issues, marriage break-ups."

Harms from gambling are not consistently treated the same way as harms arising from other industries such as tobacco and alcohol, he said, with the latter routinely screened out of superannuation investments.

Australians losing billions to gambling

Australians are losing more money per capita to gambling than any other country — about $32 billion a year.

A spokesperson for Australian Super said apart from its Socially Aware option, the fund does not apply investment screens to gambling companies.

"We invest to help members achieve their best financial position in retirement," they said.

"We engage with certain ASX-listed companies either directly or with other investors to better understand their initiatives to deliver responsible gaming practices and appropriate governance practices."

Thomas called gambling a "blind spot" for superannuation companies.

Super funds could make more ethical choices about where they put their money, he said, noting gambling investment was a relatively small part of their overall investments, which may range in the hundreds of billions of dollars.

"There is so much opportunity out there for them to do the right thing," he said.

Thomas urged those who were concerned to contact their super fund to find out how much they were investing in gambling.

"We're not looking to ban gambling; we just think it's a harmful legal adult product and should be regulated," he said.

"But we do think it should be shunned by the investment community, particularly super funds, particularly because we're all made to contribute to these super funds."

Gambling reforms announced

Gambling reforms and government support for affected Australians were announced earlier this year.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has confirmed his government intends to table laws to limit gambling ads across various forms of media, including on online platforms unless users have been verified to be over the age of 18.

The federal budget itself has also identified specific measures.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers said $39 million has been set aside over the next four years to improve the availability and reach of financial counselling services, while $28.7 million will be spent on BetStop, in part to strengthen data-matching systems.

The government plans to spend $22.4 million to develop a national online awareness campaign to encourage people affected by gambling harms to seek support.

Readers seeking support for gambling concerns can contact the National Gambling Helpline on 1800 858 858 for free, professional and confidential support 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. More information is available at www.gamblinghelponline.org.au

title: 2026 Census: When it is, how to complete it and who must take part
text:
Census night is Tuesday 11 August, and every person in Australia — including Norfolk Island, the Cocos (Keeling) Islands and Christmas Island — must be counted.

The Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) conducts a Census of Population and Housing every five years to gather what officials describe as a snapshot of the nation.

It includes up to 66 questions spanning a person's ancestry, religious affiliation, sex recorded at birth and even how they commuted to work that day.

The last Census was held on 10 August 2021.

Emily Walter, a Census national spokesperson at the ABS, said many people will be completing the Census for the first time.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"There's a lot of Australians who won't have done a Census before, and this is really important to make sure that people are aware and understand what a Census is, and why it's important," she said.

How do you complete the Census?

From late July, households will receive instructions on how to complete the Census. Australians can complete the form as soon as they receive instructions, provided they know where they will be on Census night.

Most people will receive a letter with instructions to complete the Census online. However, Australians can also request a paper form if they prefer.

The Census form is available in English. The ABS provides translated information and interpreting services for different languages. Source: Supplied / Australian Bureau of Statistics

Generally, one person per household will complete the Census, but separate forms can be requested for privacy reasons.

Who must be included in the Census?

The Census must be completed by everyone in the country, including Australian residents, international visitors, students, and people on temporary visas.

READ MORE The questions that will change in Australia's 2026 Census

Walter said the ABS expects a response from every person in the country on Census night.

"Sometimes people forget to put babies on their form. So, it's really important that absolutely everyone is reflected in the Census," she said.

What help is available to complete the Census?

Some people living in remote areas of Australia, or experiencing homelessness, can complete the Census with the assistance of field staff.

The ABS has hired around 30,000 field staff to support participation. In 2021, field staff travelled more than 10 million kilometres across Australia.

Liz Allen, a demographer from the Australian National University's Centre for Social Policy Research, said: "That especially includes people in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities where ABS data collectors actually go out in force, and they ... talk to folks about the Census, what it means and to answer any relevant questions."

Information about the Census is also accessible in 25 languages, with free translation and interpreting services available for people completing the Census.

What if you're travelling on Census night?

If a person is travelling within Australia on 11 August, they are still expected to complete the Census.

This includes anyone travelling overnight on a long-distance train, bus, or aircraft (including red-eye flights between Perth and the east coast), and on long-haul transport vehicles that are parked or in motion.

International visitors to Australia are also expected to complete the Census. The form is available at hotels and motels, as well as online in places with internet access. Source: AAP / Joel Carrett

People must include information about where they are staying or whether they are in transit.

If an Australian is overseas on 11 August, they do not need to complete the Census.

If someone is completing the Census form at their usual address, the person travelling needs to be marked as 'away' but usually living at the dwelling.

Can you be fined for not completing the Census?

The Census is compulsory under the 1905 Census and Statistics Act.

If a person does not complete the Census when directed, they will receive a formal written direction to do so. If they refuse, they can be prosecuted and fined up to $364 a day.

Knowingly providing false or misleading statements is an offence and can result in a fine of up to $3,640, according to the ABS.

title: Shady's back: New twist in Eminem's legal stoush with Australian swimwear brand
text:
In Brief The rapper had until 22 July to launch an appeal against the finding in favour of the Australian beach brand.

The appeal drags out the battle over the US rapper's intellectual property in Australia.

Lawyers for United States rapper Marshall Mathers, better known as Eminem, have filed an appeal after losing a trademark battle against Australian beach brand Swim Shady.

The Australian Registrar of Trade Marks initially ruled in favour of Swim Shady in early July, giving the rapper's legal team until 22 July to lodge an appeal.

The rapper had launched the dispute over similarities to his alter ego 'Slim Shady' — a persona Mathers developed to give voice to his more rage-fuelled raps, such as his controversial 1999 Slim Shady LP album.

The Australian beach brand was founded in 2024 by former South Sydney Rabbitohs executive Jeremy Scott and partner Elizabeth Afrakoff, and makes beach shades and accessories.

Documents were lodged with the Federal Court of Australia's Victoria Registry on Wednesday afternoon.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The rapper's legal team, Davies Collison Cave, has been contacted for comment.

Hi! My name is...

Eminem's legal team claimed Mathers has used the word 'Shady' in his promotion since 1999, with the rapper launching an associated clothing brand called Shady Limited.

In Australia, Eminem has owned the trademarks "Shady" and "Shady Limited" for use in merchandise since 2002.

It wasn't until January 2025, however, that the rapper filed for trademark rights to "Slim Shady" — a month after the launch of the Australian company.

Intellectual property law firm Meyer West IP, acting for Swim Shady, filed a 'no-use' application for a partial removal of the rapper's trademarks, arguing that they hadn't been used in Australia for the previous three years.

The judge was not satisfied Eminem demonstrated sufficient control over his image and his business enterprises, and saw no evidence of "direct use" of his trademarks in Australia during the three-year period.

The US mogul was ordered to pay Swim Shady's legal costs.

"Elizabeth and I are delighted with today's decision," founder Scott told Rolling Stone in a statement at the time.

"While this is an important milestone for Swim Shady, it is one step in the broader trade mark proceedings, and we recognise there are still matters to be resolved."

SBS News has contacted Swim Shady for comment.

'Eminem Esque'

In 2017, Eminem took New Zealand's National Party to the Wellington High Court over their use of his track 'Lose Yourself' in a political campaign.

The High Court ordered the National Party to pay NZD$600,000 ($499,000) in damages to Eminem after using a version of the iconic guitar riff, drum beat and sparse piano melody of Lose Yourself in a re-election campaign for former prime minister John Key.

The production music track was called "Eminem Esque".

The battle made it to US late-night talk show Last Week Tonight, where host John Oliver called it "unquestionably the lamest rap beef since Lil Wayne's five-year feud with William H Macy".

US singer Katy Perry lost a 12-year legal battle against Australian fashion designer Katie Perry this year. Source: AAP / Europa Press

Eminem isn't the only celebrity to launch a trademark battle in Australia.

An 18-year trademark dispute between pop singer Katy Perry and Australian fashion line Katie Perry ended in 2026 after the High Court found the designer was not in breach of trademark laws.

The pop singer initially won her case in the Federal Court, only to lose on appeal.

title: 'Truth-teller': Rebel Wilson victorious in defamation trial brought by The Deb co-star
text:
In brief Rebel Wilson's legal battle with co-star Charlotte MacInnes has ended.

Wilson repeatedly denied MacInnes' claims that she had defamed her former co-star.

After a blistering dispute that spanned continents and years, Hollywood A-lister Rebel Wilson has been found not to have defamed her co-star in a series of social media posts.

The Pitch Perfect star was sued by Australian actor Charlotte MacInnes, who played the lead role in Wilson's directorial debut film The Deb.

MacInnes claimed Wilson defamed her in a series of social media posts that implied she was a liar and a sellout who walked back a sexual harassment complaint to further her career.

The posts alleged the young actor confided in Wilson — and later recanted — that she felt uncomfortable when the film's co-producer Amanda Ghost asked to have a shower and a bath together.

MacInnes denied making or retracting a complaint, insisting she had not been uncomfortable when the pair shared a bath in their swimwear after Ghost suffered a medical episode in September 2023.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Two months after the conclusion of the hotly contested court fight in the Federal Court, Justice Elizabeth Raper found in favour of Wilson on Wednesday.

"MacInnes has failed to establish that the publications have caused or were likely to cause serious harm," she said.

MacInnes' barrister Sue Chrysanthou accused Wilson of a "complete revision of history" littered with dishonesty during her emphatic closing address in May.

"She is a fantastical liar who has made up terrible, terrible allegations about multiple people and her own witnesses have discredited her," she said.

"We say Ms Wilson cannot be believed on anything she has said."

But Wilson repeatedly denied accusations she fabricated the complaint or raised it for leverage during disputes with her co-producers over the film's budget and writing credit.

"I am known as a truth-teller," she declared during the hearing.

That was what she had been doing when she posted the stories to her 11 million followers, the Bridesmaids star said.

"I don't classify it as an attack, I classify it as telling the truth," she told the court.

Her barrister, Dauid Sibtain, said his client had "no motive to lie" about something that would endanger her film by driving a wedge between her lead actor and a producer.

Instead, he accused MacInnes of dishonestly withdrawing the complaint about Ghost to ensure her relationship with the producer continued to be fruitful.

MacInnes had received a lead role in a stage production and a six-figure record deal that were linked to Ms Ghost, he argued, and she wanted to ensure she continued to rise.

"Ms MacInnes is someone who gave false evidence under oath," he said.

"(She) has suffered no harm, let alone serious harm."

The young actor sought aggravated damages for the harm allegedly wrought by the posts, including anxiety and a period without work.
