title: The age Australians think is 'too old' to still live with your parents
text:
A new survey has revealed the age Australians think is "too old" to still be living at home — and the findings land as rents climb and rental vacancies stay tight.

The survey, from financial comparison site Finder, asked 1,010 Australians to weigh in on how old was "too old" to be still living in the family home, with the average Australian saying 31-years-old was the cut-off date.

It found gen z and baby boomers held the harshest views, both agreeing that 30 was too old to still be living with family.

Millennials and gen x were slightly more forgiving, nominating 32 as the cut-off.

Parents of under 18s were also stricter (30) than those without children (32).

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

But Finder's personal finance expert Sarah Megginson says those numbers don't reflect the financial reality many Australians are living through.

"We know renters are doing it tough — Finder's data shows 47 per cent of Aussie tenants struggled to pay their rent in June," Megginson told SBS News.

"In many suburbs, renters are spending half of their weekly income or more just on rent, and after other bills are paid there’s not much left over for groceries and simply living."

The accepted benchmark for rental stress is spending 30 per cent of income on rent — a threshold Megginson says many Australians would now consider "welcome relief" by comparison.

A new survey suggests 31-years-old is the cut-off date for living in the family home. Source: SBS News

She says the housing crisis is pushing back the traditional markers of adulthood.

"The traditional milestones of adulthood are being pushed further back, with many people delaying moving out, buying a home and starting a family," she said.

It's not necessarily a new trend, either — though data does suggest it's accelerated in recent years.

A July report from research firm McCrindle found young Australians were living with their parents for longer. In 2006, 60 per cent of 19-year-olds lived at home — but by 2021, that figure climbed to 70 per cent.

Their analysis found that young Australians were being challenged with high levels of student debt and a competitive housing market — with some returning to the family nest after moving out to accelerate savings for a housing deposit.

Almost three in five Australians (57 per cent) said they're open to or would welcome living in a multi-generational home with their family — defined as three or more generations living under the same roof.

Twelve per cent currently live in a multi-generational home, McCrindle found, while 32 per cent have done so in the past — with those who speak a language other than English at home significantly more likely to live in this way.

People living in multi-generational households previously told SBS' The Feed the arrangement can help families share costs and caring responsibilities, while providing greater security as relatives age. But some said Australian homes were not well designed to accommodate several generations.

Different cultural backgrounds also played a role in attitudes towards multi-generational living, they said.

Rents high, vacancies low

The pressure to move out of home is playing out against a rental market that's becoming steadily less forgiving.

Nationally, rents rose 3.08 per cent quarter-on-quarter in June and 6.35 per cent over the year, according to REA Group, taking the median weekly rent for a dwelling to $670.

Perth recorded the steepest annual rise, up 10.29 per cent to a $750 median, followed by Hobart (up 9.09 per cent, to $600) and Darwin (up 7.69 per cent, to $700).

Nationally, the weekly median rent for a dwelling is sitting at $670 — and $690 across combined capital cities. Source: SBS News

Sydney remains the most expensive capital, with rents up 6.67 per cent to an $800 median, ahead of Brisbane ($695, up 6.92 per cent), the ACT ($650, up 4.84 per cent), Adelaide ($625, up 4.17 per cent) and Melbourne ($600, up 5.26 per cent).

Vacancies are just as tight.

The national vacancy rate ticked up slightly in June, to 1.26 per cent, but that's still far below where it sat five years ago — down 37 per cent nationally, and 47 per cent in capital cities specifically.

Nationally, vacancy rates are sitting at 1.26 per cent — a slight increase monthly, but still down 37 per cent over the last five years. Source: SBS News

Darwin (0.70 per cent) and Hobart (0.72 per cent) have the least available rental stock of any capital, while Melbourne (1.51 per cent) and the ACT (1.50 per cent) have the most breathing room.

Sydney's vacancy rate sits at 1.30 per cent, though it recorded the biggest monthly jump of any capital, up 0.14 percentage points.

Why staying home longer is becoming the norm

REA Group economist Luc Redman says that squeeze is precisely what's keeping young Australians at home for longer.

"The rental market remains tight nationally despite some easing since the start of 2026," Redman told SBS News.

"The rate of rental price increases certainly would be deterring consumers from entering the rental market and looking for other options where possible."

"This has meant that younger people stay in their parents' home for longer."

He says the shift has a broader impact.

"That has a broader negative impact on the economy when economically productive young people aren't able to move closer to economic opportunities," he said.

"This has certainly shifted over the past few decades as the income of younger people hasn't been able to keep up with rent price increases."

Separately, July Cotality research found households are now devoting close to a third of their income to rent — a record high — compared to around 27 per cent five years ago.

That sits alongside July data from the OECD, which found Australians' real wages have fallen 5.1 per cent since March 2021, one of the steepest declines among developed economies — making rental hikes sting even more.

Australians born in the 1990s are the first generation on record to be no better off than the one that preceded them, according to the Productivity Commission.

Megginson says living at home longer may now be the smarter financial move.

"Living with your parents at 31 may raise eyebrows, but struggling to afford rent is becoming the norm," she said.

"Living at home longer can be a smart financial move, allowing young adults to save a deposit faster and avoid the pressure of soaring rents."

title: Former Socceroo becomes first Australian footballer to share probable CTE diagnosis
text:
Former Socceroos captain Paul Wade has revealed a diagnosis of probable chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE), a neurodegenerative disease associated with repeated head impacts.

In a statement shared by Professional Footballers Australia (PFA), Wade, who captained the Socceroos between 1990 and 1996, including at the Olympic Games and across two World Cup qualifying campaigns, says a neurologist diagnosed him with probable CTE in late 2025 after he presented with neurological symptoms in late 2024.

It makes him the first Australian footballer to disclose a diagnosis of probable CTE.

CTE is a progressive, degenerative brain disease caused by repeated head trauma, which has commonly been associated with boxers. Concerns about the risks to footballers were first raised publicly after former England striker Jeff Astle became the first British footballer found to have died from the disease. Since then, a growing body of research has revealed the link between heading the ball and increased risk of neurodegenerative disease.

While CTE is generally confirmed after death, medical specialists are able to diagnose suspected or probable cases based on an individual's background, history of repeated head knocks and symptoms of cognitive impairment.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Wade says he is sharing his diagnosis to raise awareness of concussion, repeated head impacts and brain health across all levels of football.

"Football gave me so much. It gave me a life, a career, incredible experiences and the privilege of representing Australia. But I also must be honest about the challenges I now face and the uncertainty that comes with living with a neurodegenerative condition," he says.

Receiving a diagnosis of probable CTE has been difficult. But I have decided to speak publicly because I know there may be other players and families facing similar questions.

"If sharing my experience can help someone seek support, create better education and prevention, or help the game take brain health more seriously, then it is worth doing.

"I do not want to discourage anyone from playing football, as I think there are so many positive health benefits. But we do need to understand more about the long-term effects of repeated head impacts and make sure that players, families and the game have the information and support they need to protect future generations."

As SBS News has previously reported, football associations in the United Kingdom and United States have introduced laws banning heading for young players, and have also limited the practice among older cohorts due to concerns about the cumulative impacts of heading the ball.

Read our in-depth interactive on the risks of heading in football

Football Australia, which is responsible for regulating football in the country, has not introduced any similar recommendations.

A spokesperson for Football Australia said it had established an expert project team, which includes chief medical officer Mark Jones and joint head of research and development, professor Rob Duffield, alongside a dedicated concussion research officer, to look at strategies to reduce the incidence, magnitude and burden of heading in youth football, commencing with a comprehensive literature review.

It comes as calls grow for heading to be restricted at younger ages.

The Concussion Legacy Foundation Australia believes children under 14 years old should not be heading the ball at all, and that these skills can be learned later.

Wade's neurologist, Rowena Mobbs, has recommended a reduction in heading at the junior level, and the removal of all heading for under 12s.

"We want kids entering the great sport of football today to be their brain best both on the field and throughout life. You only get one brain," Mobbs says.

"Ultimately, it's about being positive and progressive in football. As a neurologist, I call for a reduction in heading for players, including removing all heading under 12."

Mobbs commended Wade for talking openly about CTE.

"We want former players to get help as early as possible so they can live well and tackle the effect of CTE on thinking, emotion and behaviour."

She noted help is available for former players as well as their families and loved ones via ConneCTErs Australia or Dementia Australia.

PFA chief executive Beau Busch said Wade's decision to share his experience was an important act of leadership.

"Australian football must proactively pursue being Australia's safest sport. That means proactively tackling the issue of brain injury, through better policies, research, education and support for former players."

In its statement, the PFA noted it is preparing a comprehensive response to "the growing understanding of and research into concussion and chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) in football players".

"The PFA will continue to develop and pursue practical, evidence-based solutions that better protect players while ensuring the game continues to grow and thrive in Australia," Busch said.

title: 'Strong concerns': Australia meets with China following ballistic missile test
text:
Australia has raised its concerns with China over its nuclear-capable missile test in the South Pacific, noting many of the region's nations also shared the same view.

Foreign Minister Penny Wong met with her Chinese counterpart, Wang Yi, on the sidelines of ASEAN talks in the Philippines capital, Manila, on Tuesday evening.

"You speak about the importance of mutual understanding. I agree, and I expect we will discuss our concerns ... including around peace and stability in our region, safe and professional military conduct, and the importance of transparency and reassurance of intent," Wong said in opening remarks.

Wong noted the "strong concerns" expressed by many Pacific Island countries about the submarine-launched missile earlier in July.

The two foreign ministers also discussed the importance of peace and stability in the Indo-Pacific.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"You've often heard me say that we will cooperate where we can and disagree where we must, and we want to continue to engage because we believe that is in our national interest," Wong said.

Australia was only given a few hours' notice by China of the test, which was launched within hours of Prime Minister Anthony Albanese signing a new defence alliance with Fiji.

The missile is understood to have landed near Tuvalu.

Albanese slammed the launch as a "provocative act" while other Pacific nations including New Zealand, Tuvalu and Fiji condemned the incident.

Solomon Islands Prime Minister Matthew Wale was standing beside his Australian counterpart in his country's capital Honiara in early July when he said the test was "not the act of a friend".

Albanese has previously flagged a strong statement would be issued by the Pacific Islands Forum, but the group's disagreement on the wording of the text has resulted in a delay.

A foreign ministers' meeting of the forum will be held in August, where the issue is likely to be discussed.

title: 'Business-friendly socialism': What are new UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham's policies?
text:
In brief Andy Burnham was the mayor of Greater Manchester before replacing Keir Starmer as the United Kingdom's prime minister.

Burnham says he'll lead a "cost of living government", with one of his first policies cutting taxes on electricity bills.

Andy Burnham, sworn in this week as the United Kingdom's seventh prime minister in a decade, has opened his tenure with a slew of new policies, branding his own political ideology as "business-friendly socialism".

Burnham was sworn in as the UK's new prime minister on Monday, replacing Keir Starmer, who resigned following months of pressure from his own MPs after just over two years in the job.

The former Greater Manchester mayor, nicknamed the 'King of the North' for his defence of the region during his nine-year stint as mayor, used his first days in office to signal a new leftward shift for the Labour government.

On his first full day, Burnham told the inaugural meeting of his cabinet that he would lead a "cost of living government".

By the second day, his first concrete policies had arrived.

Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

What are Andy Burnham's policies?

Burnham has branded his political ideology "business-friendly socialism" — with a distinct focus on cost of living measures.

He has unveiled some early policies designed "to do some things that people will feel and feel quite quickly", especially those on the lowest incomes — adding that a longer-term plan would be set out later in the year.

On Tuesday, Burnham announced he will cut taxes on electricity bills — one of the main drivers of the UK's cost of living crisis — in his first move to ease pressures that have angered the public and helped stoke political instability.

Burnham said his government would remove the value-added tax from domestic electricity bills from 1 October, cutting about £45 ($86) from the average annual bill of about £1,860 ($3,554) that households pay.

"We're taking immediate action to cut taxes on energy bills, put more money in people's pockets and bring back hope," Burnham said in a statement, having pledged to give people more "breathing space" in their lives.

The government said it would be funded by cancelling Starmer's plan for a digital ID scheme that would have cost the taxpayer £1.8 billion ($3.44 billion) — but former minister Darren Jones said the ID program had been "unfunded".

The energy announcement is designed to show that Burnham will move quickly to improve living standards after years of stagnation.

He said on Monday his early policies might not "take all of the pressure off" people struggling with rising prices, and that his 10-year plan will be unveiled later this year.

What else is on the agenda?

During his first speech as prime minister on Monday, Burnham also said his first ambition was to end rough sleeping in the UK.

He is reportedly considering rent control measures, alongside plans to build more public housing to help bring down welfare spending.

Burnham has pledged to re-industrialise the country and push through greater public control of utilities.

He is also expected to look at possible caps on bus fares — an echo of his policy in Manchester, where riders would not pay more than £2 ($3.80) — and other measures to back up his assertion that he would "put the care of people at the heart of everything I do".

He has long supported the idea that social care should be more of a universal system, but says he wants to see a "fair and lasting" reduction to welfare spending — through improving employment support rather than cutting payment benefits.

On immigration, Burnham recently said net migration "needs to fall further", though he hasn't set a specific target.

He also intends to decentralise power away from London to other cities and regions, promising the biggest-ever "rebalancing of power".

As part of this, he will spend "at least one day a week" working in Manchester at 'No. 10 North', and will move some government operations from London to Manchester.

He also said last September that he would like to see the UK back in the European Union within his lifetime, but said he does not want to re-run a Brexit referendum at the moment.

— With additional reporting by Reuters, Agence France-Presse, and the Australian Associated Press.

title: Flights to return after 40-year hiatus as Trump promises to help Lebanon 'a lot'
text:
In brief Joseph Aoun is the first Lebanese president invited to Washington since 2009.

Aoun asked US President Donald Trump to continue backing the Lebanese army, along with support for an Israeli withdrawal.

United States President Donald Trump vowed on Wednesday to help Lebanon "a lot" as its president, Joseph Aoun, sought the US' backing for a fragile effort to disarm Hezbollah and forge a path to peace with Israel.

Meeting Aoun at the White House, Trump said Lebanon had been "a very mistreated country" that had been "hit hard" for decades.

"It's probably a dangerous place in a lot of ways," Trump told reporters. "But they love the country ... and we're going to help it. We're going to help it a lot."

Aoun, the first Lebanese president invited to Washington since 2009, asked Trump for political support and continued backing for the Lebanese army, which has begun deploying in southern areas under a US-backed framework agreement with Israel.

"We need to support the LAF," Aoun said, referring to the Lebanese Armed Forces. "Without the LAF, everything will collapse."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Aoun's nearly two-hour visit took place as Israeli troops continue to occupy a swath of the country's south and hundreds of thousands of Lebanese remain displaced following Israeli strikes. Hezbollah has rejected the government's direct talks with Israel as well as efforts by the state to disarm it.

The talks highlighted Aoun's delicate balancing act — seeking Trump's help to secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon while simultaneously facing pressure from the US and Israel to disarm Hezbollah, which remains a formidable fighting force despite recent setbacks.

The Lebanese president, a former army commander, said the military was "the backbone of security and stability" and the country's most trusted institution.

In a statement after the talks, the Lebanese presidency said Aoun stressed to Trump the "urgent need for a complete Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory," calling it "an essential step to consolidate stability and enable the Lebanese state to extend its full sovereignty exclusively through its own forces."

Flights resume

Following his meeting with Aoun, Trump said he would direct his administration to allow all US airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon— a historic policy shift more than 40 years in the making.

US flights were suspended in 1985 after the hijacking of TWA Flight 847 from Athens, Greece, en route to Rome, Italy, that was forced to land in Beirut. During the 17-day ordeal, a US Navy diver was brutally killed.

The Federal Aviation Administration and Transportation Department did not immediately comment.

Trump's visit came as the Lebanese army moved into an area of the south under a deal with Israel aimed at proving Lebanon can extend state authority and disarm Iran-backed Hezbollah.

But in a sign of the fragility of the agreement, the Lebanese army said Israeli troops fired near its soldiers deploying in a so-called "pilot zone" Israel is supposed to withdraw from.

Asked whether the US would put additional pressure on Israel to withdraw, Trump said: "We'll be looking at that."

Lebanon is under pressure to prove that it can and will disarm Hezbollah, which Israel has set as a precondition for ending its occupation in southern Lebanon.

The "pilot zones" where the Lebanese army is deploying are primarily located just outside territory Israeli forces invaded after Hezbollah opened rocket fire on Israel and drew Lebanon into the Middle East war in March.

The army said on Tuesday it had begun deploying to one of three pilot zones, the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiya. Israel's military said it would be "adjusting its force posture" in one pilot area.

Hours later, however, the Lebanese army said Israeli forces "opened fire in the vicinity of these units," adding that the incident would hinder the deployment.

The Israel Defense Forces (IDF) said it had only fired "warning shots into the air" after Lebanese soldiers entered an area that was "not part of the pilot area".

"The Lebanese Armed Forces personnel withdrew from the area, and no injuries were reported," it added.

'Terrorist infrastructure'

Aoun has staunchly criticised Hezbollah for forcing Lebanon into the war and has vowed since taking office in early 2025 to bring all weapons under state control.

But Hezbollah has repeatedly rejected laying down its arms, and the group has flatly opposed both the deal and the Israel-Lebanon negotiations that underpin it.

Aoun said the framework agreement did not require parliamentary endorsement and was a presidential prerogative under Lebanon's constitution.

US secretary of state Marco Rubio said after meeting Aoun on Sunday that Lebanon would "never truly get peace" as long as Hezbollah remained armed.

Rubio also praised what he called the Lebanese government's effort to reclaim sovereignty, disarm Hezbollah and dismantle its "terrorist infrastructure."

Aoun's push comes after Israeli strikes in the latest conflict killed more than 4,300 people in Lebanon, according to Lebanese authorities.

The violence has dropped off sharply since the signing of a framework agreement between the United States and Iran last month and the Lebanon-Israel deal.

Trump also used the meeting to warn Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels against following through on threats to blockade Saudi ports.

"If something like that happens, we take care of it," Trump told reporters. "We've done that with the Houthis before, and we haven't heard from them in a while since we did what we did originally."

title: Bigger, richer and messier: The World Cup that reshaped FIFA's showpiece
text:
In brief Cape Verde's run helped justify FIFA's expanded 48-team format.

The tournament delivered record crowds and revenue, but controversy followed FIFA.

The biggest World Cup in history came to a close on Monday, with Spain defeating Argentina to lift football's biggest prize.

On many measures, the tournament was a resounding success. But it was also a World Cup of contradictions.

Cape Verde's remarkable run became the tournament's fairy-tale story, vindicating FIFA's decision to expand the format to 48 teams.

Millions of fans flocked to the United States, Canada and Mexico, packing out sold-out stadiums with the hosts receiving near-universal praise for their hospitality.

Norwegian fans took over the New York subway for the iconic "Norwegian Row", while Scotland's Tartan Army drank Boston's bars dry, becoming among the tournament's most memorable fan stories.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

But the World Cup was also marked by controversy.

US President Donald Trump's intervention over a one-match suspension handed to USA striker Folarin Balogun drew widespread criticism.

US President Donald Trump (left) personally asked FIFA president Gianni Infantino (right) to review a one-game suspension handed to a USA player. Source: Bloomberg / Annabelle Gordon/ via Getty Images

The treatment of the Iranian team and Somali referee Omar Artan, as well as increased prices and debate over favouritism, also fuelled criticism of FIFA.

So what defined this World Cup? And are FIFA's biggest changes here to stay?

FIFA's most lucrative World Cup

FIFA was undoubtedly the biggest winner.

The president of footballs governing body, Gianni Infantino, on Saturday declared it "the greatest FIFA World Cup of all time", surpassing all expectations.

And he had good reason to be happy.

While FIFA is yet to release its official financial results, The Guardian reported the tournament generated a record $21.4 billion in revenue — far exceeding pre-tournament forecasts.

Listen To Podcast · English · Sports World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast World Cup 2026 wrap: Highlights, controversies and the future 21/07/2026 14:37

Spain beats Lionel Messi's Argentina to win 2026 World Cup 20/07/2026 14:03

England defeats France to claim bronze, Mbappé leads World Cup goal record 19/07/2026 10:08

Will FIFA keep hydration breaks at the next World Cup? 17/07/2026 16:17

Heartbreak for England as Argentina heads to World Cup final against Spain 16/07/2026 12:12 View more

However, that success was often at the expense of fans.

Those who made the trip needed deep pockets, with ticket prices skyrocketing with the introduction of dynamic pricing and an inflated resale market that saw FIFA take a 30 per cent cut.

Concerns high prices would result in empty seats proved largely unfounded.

FIFA said more than 6.6 million spectators attended matches up to and including the semi-finals, with stadiums reaching 99.7 per cent capacity — the highest attendance rate in World Cup history.

Viewers at home were also subject to more advertising after the introduction of controversial hydration breaks.

But that did not deter viewers, with FIFA claiming 5.2 billion people — nearly two-thirds of the world's population — engaged with the World Cup at least once.

University of Sydney sports studies expert Dr Steve Georgakis said he did not expect future tournaments to be any less expensive.

"One of the biggest negative aspects of it is that it's really taking the sport away from the working classes, the people whose communities are besotted by soccer," he told SBS News.

Expanded format

The biggest structural change was the expansion from 32 teams to 48.

Critics argued the larger tournament — there were 104 matches compared with 64 in recent tournaments — would reduce the quality of the competition and produce more lop-sided matches.

Instead, World Cup debutantes such as minnows Curaçao, Cape Verde, Uzbekistan and Jordan proved them wrong.

Aside from Curaçao's 7-1 defeat to Germany, none of the debutantes suffered heavy losses and each of them scored at least one goal.

Cape Verde's World Cup campaign became one of the highlights of the tournament. Source: AAP / Fotoarena/ Sipa USA

Cape Verde, with a population of about 500,000, defied all expectations, holding eventual champions Spain to a scoreless draw in their opening game.

The island nation qualified for the Round of 32, where they took runner-ups Argentina to extra time before losing 3-2 in what was one of the most exciting matches of the tournament.

Former FIFA referee Christina Unkel said she thought the expanded format was a boon for smaller countries, which benefited from playing in large tournaments.

"The new teams to the tournament showed that they're there to play ball," she told SBS News.

"When you open up opportunities and slots for teams to make the World Cup, there is more funding that happens in those federations to be competitive.

"You continue to grow and increase the game because they say, 'Hey, I actually have an opportunity', and it's not just the powerhouse football clubs."

Georgakis agreed the expansion had been vindicated.

"In all of the group stage matches, every game except for a couple, right to the death, the teams were playing for something," he told SBS News.

"There were no real weak teams. International soccer has come to the point now where the smaller nations really trouble the very big nations, and we saw that."

FIFA has discussed expanding the format to 64 teams for the 2030 World Cup, though the proposal is divisive.

The controversies

However there were plenty of scandals.

The biggest centred on Trump's intervention over Balogun's suspension.

The suspension of Folarin Balogun's one-match ban following his red card against Bosnia and Herzegovina rocked the footballing world. Source: AAP / Benjamin Fanjoy / EPA

The USA striker had been due to miss the Round of 16 against Belgium after receiving a red card against Bosnia and Herzegovina, resulting in a one-match suspension.

But the one-match ban was inexplicably suspended for a year by FIFA, allowing Balogun to play. Trump later confirmed he had personally asked Infantino to review the case.

Infantino defended the decision, saying it was made by an independent judicial panel and his conversation with Trump had not influenced the outcome.

But the decision received widespread condemnation from former players, journalists and other footballing bodies.

Some experts warned it could set a "dangerous precedent" as political leaders sought to influence football's governing body.

"That's a real sour point," Georgakis said.

"It's very clear there that Donald Trump had a very unsettling relationship with FIFA."

Another controversy emerged before the tournament had even started.

Despite holding valid travel documents and FIFA accreditation, Somali referee Omar Artan was denied entry to the United States on arrival in Miami and missed the tournament.

Iran also accused organisers of unfair treatment after travel restrictions forced their players to fly between their base camp in Mexico and the United States for each match.

Argentina, meanwhile, faced accusations of favourable refereeing after their 3-2 Round of 16 victory over Egypt.

Egypt filed a complaint against FIFA after the match, arguing that an early goal was incorrectly ruled out for a foul in the build-up and saying they should have been awarded a penalty in a separate play.

Egypt's head coach Hossam Hassan was highly critical of the officiating in his side's loss to Argentina. Source: Getty / Buda Mendes

But Unkel said the incidents were different from an officiating perspective and warned against interpreting difficult refereeing decisions as evidence of bias.

"The Egypt-Argentina was what I would call as a perfect storm," she said.

"It's on the heels of sentiments that people had even before the tournament started that Argentina is historically benefiting from these decisions and these calls.

"You're going into that ... even as a referee, knowing that, everyone's looking for something.

"The nuance of officiating subjective decisions is incredibly important to understand, and it's very easy to paint broad brush strokes versus looking at the details."

Georgakis said controversy was almost inevitable at an event as large as the World Cup.

"A World Cup without controversies is not a World Cup," he said.

But he said he would ultimately remember it as a success.

"I'll remember it for one thing, and that is the fact that we had 48 teams taking part in 104 games," he said.

"The World Cup has been ramped up, and you can argue that it is now truly an inclusive World Cup."

title: Two years into the government's social housing push, a new report says it's well behind target
text:
In brief A new report has suggested that the government is well behind its social housing targets.

The government is now two years into a five-year target period.

Less than four per cent of the social homes promised by the Albanese government's signature social housing fund have been delivered, two years into a five-year target period.

As of May 2026, 1432 of the Housing Australia Future Fund's target of 40,000 new social and affordable homes had been delivered, the Australian National Audit Office found in a report released on Tuesday.

The $10 billion fund's forecast to hit its target by mid-2029 faced "considerable uncertainty" as of June, the auditor-general found.

It's another setback for the government's broader housing supply agenda, with the construction industry about a year behind on its target to deliver 1.2 million new homes in the five years to June 2029.

Meanwhile, the social housing waitlist has grown from 155,000 to 169,000 in the 10 years to June 2024.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

While Treasury was largely effective in designing the fund, the department had failed to take clear responsibility for delivering the program and provided little transparency about how it was progressing, the audit found.

Although funding applications opened in January 2024, Treasury had not finalised delivery arrangements until May 2026.

"The delayed finalisation of most governance and oversight arrangements has limited the ability of Treasury to systematically monitor and manage program risks and performance over time," the auditor-general said.

"Once finalised, Treasury has not always implemented governance and oversight arrangements as intended, with sometimes out-of-date and inconsistent arrangements.

"Risk management arrangements are insufficient, including for shared risks."

The fund works by giving money to providers to either build new homes or purchase newly constructed homes from developers.

Once built, homes are subsidised by the government to provide tenants below-market rates for 25 years, at a cost of about $393,000 per dwelling in today's money.

Of the 1432 homes delivered by the fund, 762 were new homes constructed by housing providers, and 670 were newly built by private developers and purchased by a housing provider before, during or immediately after development.

The audit made five recommendations to improve governance, risk management, performance review and transparency of the program.

Housing Minister Clare O'Neil welcomed the report findings and accepted all recommendations.

"Importantly, the report found Treasury's design of the Housing Australia Future Fund was largely effective and supported by largely sound policy advice," she said.

"This program is being delivered in one of the toughest construction markets in decades.

"As building and financing costs increased, Treasury identified those pressures early, and the government acted to protect our commitment to deliver more social and affordable homes."

Opposition housing spokesman Andrew Bragg said the report laid bare how the fund failed to deliver.

"Only a handful of houses have been built, and just under half are not new," he said.

"Buying homes is not building homes. It does nothing to fix Australia's housing supply crisis."

title: Dalaroo geophysics unlocks thick Greenland rare earths potential
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Dalaroo geophysics unlocks thick Greenland rare earths potential Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Murray Ward July 23, 2026 — 12:58pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Dalaroo Metals says it has peered into the depths beneath the surface of its Blue Lagoon rare earths project in Greenland and liked what it saw, with a geophysical survey confirming unconsolidated sediment packages up to 50 metres in thickness. A total of twenty-one ground penetrating radar (GPR) survey lines were completed across five priority shoreline target areas, offering the first detailed, three-dimensional picture of the project’s sedimentary basin. Eastern sediment-filled valley interpreted as a source-to-sink transport pathway into the basin at Dalaroo’s Blue Lagoon project in Greenland. The work, combined with an echo sounder survey, has confirmed extensive sediment accumulations within the primary rare earth-bearing shoreline areas. The results are a significant step forward for Dalaroo’s sediment-hosted exploration model, which centres on rare earth-enriched alkaline granites eroding into a contained lagoon environment.

Advertisement

‘Every phase of exploration continues to strengthen our geological understanding of Blue Lagoon and increase our confidence.’ Dalaroo Metals chief executive officer John Morgan For these types of deposits, the thickness and continuity of the mineralised sediments are fundamental controls on their potential size. The survey demonstrated that sediment layers in several locations extend beyond the GPR system’s 50m penetration limit, leaving these deeper zones open. This suggests that even greater accumulations of valuable rare earth mineralisation might be lurking below. The company says one of the most significant outcomes was the identification of numerous previously unrecognised sediment-filled valleys extending beyond the current exploration footprint. Dalaroo believes these valleys represent additional “source-to-sink” transport pathways capable of carrying and concentrating rare earth-bearing heavy minerals from the surrounding source rocks.

Advertisement

The discovery lends considerable weight to the growing view that Blue Lagoon is part of a much bigger sedimentary system than originally recognised and extends well beyond the areas currently targeted by exploration. In addition to the GPR work, ten bathymetric survey lines were completed across the lagoon itself, delivering the first detailed model of the lagoon floor. This will help the company in interpreting sediment transport pathways and the depositional environments responsible for concentrating the valuable heavy mineral sands. Dalaroo Metals chief executive officer John Morgan said: “Our 2025 exploration program confirmed widespread rare earth mineralisation within shoreline sediments. This latest work now demonstrates that those sedimentary environments are substantially thicker than originally understood, with sediment accumulations extending to approximately 50 metres in several priority areas.” Whilst Greenland is shaping up to be the company’s headline act, Dalaroo is also building a solid portfolio of gold assets in West Africa’s highly endowed Birimian greenstone belt.

Advertisement

At its Bondoukou gold project in Côte d’Ivoire, recent soil sampling defined a significant 7km-long gold anomaly at the Gold Ridge prospect. The work returned a headline-grabbing peak soil value of 23.26 grams per tonne (g/t) gold and outlined multiple consistent anomalies that remain open along strike. The project sits in the Siguiri Basin, a prolific region that hosts several multi-million-ounce gold deposits. The company also holds the Kokoumbo project in Côte d’Ivoire, which came with a suite of historical drill hits that were never followed up, including a stellar 7.5-metre intersection grading 16g/t gold from surface and another hit of 4.5m at 4.22g/t gold. Back on home soil in Western Australia, the company holds the Lyons River project in the Gascoyne province, where it announced a gold system discovery in April 2024. With the geophysical fieldwork in Greenland now complete, Dalaroo says its focus will turn to integrating the new 3D model with its geological mapping and pending assay results from its recent comprehensive field program. This will allow management to prioritise future auger drilling, bulk sampling and metallurgical studies.

Advertisement

With its geological model at Blue Lagoon now significantly strengthened, Dalaroo has provided a strong technical foundation for the next phase of exploration. The GPR survey has essentially confirmed the “sink” is not only present, it is also substantially bigger and more complex than first thought. As Dalaroo works to integrate the new data and await key assay results, the market will likely be watching to see just how big this Greenlandic critical minerals system could become. title: Behind the glitter and excess lurks murder and savagery
text:
Sydney Opera House, July 21

It is the “curse” – la maledizione – that drives the drama of Verdi’s Rigoletto. We hear it in the overture’s first portentous notes and in Rigoletto’s despairing cry at the close. Rigoletto believes it ultimately derives from his ugliness, the hunchback that condemns him to a life of humiliation and corrosive bitterness, making him over-protective of the one thing of beauty in his life, his daughter, Gilda.

This classic production by the late Elijah Moshinsky transfers that ugliness to the world at large, in particular, the Duke of Mantua’s court, the centrepiece of designer Michael Yeargan’s rotating set. Creating a surprising sense of depth and space on the Joan Sutherland Theatre’s limited stage, it juxtaposes a palace of glittering debauchery, the false sanctuary of Rigoletto’s home and the seedy tavern of the final tragedy, all connected by murderous dark alleyways.

The Opera Australia Chorus evidently enjoy their debauchery in Rigoletto. Keith Saunders

For this revival, Dalibor Jenis sings the title role with a voice of strength and depth, grained with worry and fierce in self-loathing. He is impressive throughout, but never more so than in the crucial reconciliation scene with the seduced Gilda in Act 2 where impotent frustration over his fate mixes with fragile tenderness.

This was also Gemma Nha’s finest moment as Gilda. Here she displayed a wide emotional and tonal range, beginning Tutte le feste al tempio with winning quietness, before rising to thrilling passion. Earlier she had sung Caro Nome in Act 1 with a bright penetrating sound and a tight vibrato that crazed the tonal surface.

Her dramatic persona moved lightly between pertness, filial loyalty and youthful yearning. Kang Wang as the Duke also emphasised lightness rather than power, his attractive tenor sound and strong command of the upper range creating an autocrat of insouciant suaveness rather than overbearing menace.

La donna e mobile had buoyant clarity. Such a nice young man! With an effectively threatening edge to his low notes, David Parkin was an aptly brutal Sparafucile (literally “shoot gun” – you get the idea). As his seducing sister, Maddalena, Sian Sharp sang with colour and strength, injecting an important element of human warmth into the sound.

Gennadi Dubinsky, as Monterone the wronged father, was imposing though slightly less searing in delivering the plot’s actual curse in Act 1 than in his portentous and incisive power in shackles in Act 2.

Dalibor Jenis as Rigoletto and Gemma Nha as Gilda. Keith Saunders

The Opera Australia Chorus seemed to enjoy their depravity, and sang with an energised, full sound, the male members delivering conspiratorial whispered precision in the kidnap scene and a quite passable soft-shoe shuffle.

Under conductor Sesto Quatrini, the Opera Australia Orchestra highlighted the shudders and flashes of Verdi’s highly original orchestration with discreet balance. Simon Meadows (Marullo), Virgilio Marino (Borsa), Jonathan Alley (Ceprano), Dominica Matthews (a haughtily, ungracious Giovanna), Jennifer Black (Countess Ceprano) and Katherine Wiles (Page) adeptly filled out a cast in which nobody acts particularly admirably.

This production is a gem of Opera Australia’s repertoire though it doesn’t seek to problematise male possessiveness, which is the story’s real curse.

title: ASX loses ground on back of jobs data; Miners, James Hardie advance
text:
Advertisement

Updated BusinessMarketsWorld markets ASX loses ground on back of jobs data; Miners, James Hardie advance Staff writers Updated July 23, 2026 — 12:45pm ,first published July 23, 2026 — 5:18am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The Australian sharemarket has pared some of its strong early gains after the release of the nation’s latest job market figures kept the chances of an interest rate rise on the table. The S&P/ASX 200 was up 56 points, or 0.6 per cent, to 8879 as of 12.42pm AEST, led by the mining and financial sectors, which make up more than half of the local market. Wall Street swung between gains and losses throughout the session. Bloomberg The Australian Bureau of Statistics this morning reported that in June the jobless rate increased by 0.1 percentage points to 4.4 per cent. The under-employment rate also lifted, climbing to 6.5 per cent. The bureau said 76,300 jobs were created last month of which 47,000 were part-time. The ASX added 0.3 per cent on Wednesday. The Australian dollar was trading at US70.16¢.

Advertisement

In major corporate news, Macquarie Group said this morning its chief executive Shemara Wikramanayake is retiring as its CEO after eight years in the job, to be replaced by the head of its banking arm, Greg Ward. The investment banking giant said Wikramanayake, who is 64 and has been chief executive since late 2018, will retire from November this year. Ward, 58, is a Macquarie veteran who has run the group’s banking and financial services arm, which houses its rapidly-growing retail bank, since 2013. He joined Macquarie in 1996 and previously served as chief financial officer for 14 years. Related Article Opinion

Middle East at war The real oil shock from the Iran war might be just beginning Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist The shares were little moved on the news, up 0.5 per cent in early trade. Meanwhile, the big four banks all advanced, with CBA up 1.5 per cent, Westpac up 1.3 per cent, National Australia Bank up 1.6 per cent and ANZ Bank up 1.1 per cent. James Hardie jumped 6.7 per cent after the building materials maker - which makes most of its earnings in the US - said its June quarter sales beat its own forecasts, coming in at between $US1.45 billion ($2.1 billion) and $US1.48 billion, thanks to stronger-than-expected sales of its home sidings.

Advertisement

Mining giants BHP, Rio Tinto and Fortescue extended their strong run from Wednesday, up 1.4 per cent, 0.6 per cent and 1.7 per cent, respectively, in early afternoon trade. The gold miners also continued to rally as the precious metal extended its gains, supported by dip-buyers who scooped it up after prices had drifted lower in recent weeks on concerns that US inflationary pressure from higher oil prices will prompt the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates. Bullion rose as much as 2.1 per cent overnight to climb above $US4160 an ounce, after advancing 1.7 per cent the previous session. Gold miner Northern Star Resources was up 2.7 per cent, Evolution Mining gained 2.9 per cent and Newmont rose 1.5 per cent. Energy stocks had another strong morning as oil prices continued to climb, hitting a six-week high. Brent crude spiked in post-settlement trading overnight, rising near $US95 a barrel after Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels said they struck two Saudi tankers. Local oil and gas giant Woodside rose 0.5 per cent, and Santos added 1 per cent, even as it cut its production guidance for the second quarter due to challenges in the ramp-up of its Barossa and Pikka projects, and the timing of cargo receipts. Coal producers Yancoal and Whitehaven were up 1.4 and 2.6 per cent, respectively. Real estate investment trusts led early gains on the ASX, with warehouse and IT data centre owner Goodman Group up 1.9 per cent, and shopping centre landlords Scentre, Vicinity and Stockland up 2.1 per cent, 0.6 per cent and 0.7 per cent, respectively.

Advertisement

Tech stocks slumped, with software makers Xero and WiseTech falling 3.9 per cent and 4.5 per cent, while family tracking app Life 360 lost 5.1 per cent. The ASX’s early gains come even as Wall Street was drifting in mixed trading and oil prices climbed another 3 per cent to a six-week high as fighting continues in the war with Iran. The S&P 500 slipped 0.1 per cent, coming off its best day in three weeks, after swinging between modest losses and gains for much of the day. The Dow Jones closed flat and the Nasdaq composite lost 0.6 per cent. Tesla released its results after the closing bell, reporting negative free cash flow in the second quarter for the first time in more than two years as the Elon Musk-led EV maker accelerated spending on AI infrastructure, battery capacity, robotaxis and next-generation manufacturing. Tesla reported negative free cash flow of $US1.1 billion, compared with analysts’ expectation for cash burn of $US3.3 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG. EV sales in the quarter helped assuage some fears for now. Tesla delivered 480,126 vehicles in the second quarter, above Wall Street expectations and up from 384,122 vehicles a year earlier. Shares were down 3.3 per cent in extended trading. Alphabet topped Wall Street estimates for its second-quarter revenue, buoyed by continued strong demand for its cloud computing services from enterprises worldwide.

Advertisement

Revenue at Google Cloud rose 82 per cent to $US24.8 billion during the quarter ended June, accelerating from the 63 per cent jump reported in the preceding three months. Analysts on average expected a 64 per cent increase, according to data compiled by LSEG. Shares of the company were flat in extended trading. The majority of stocks in the S&P 500 rose after more companies reported strong profits for the spring. Expectations are high for the reports, and companies will need to match them after their stock prices already neared records on anticipation for them. Philip Morris International rose 3.3 per cent after the seller of Marlboro cigarettes reported stronger profit and revenue for the latest quarter than expected. Its shipments of smoke-free products rose 7.5 per cent. AT&T climbed 3.5 per cent after reporting a stronger profit than analysts expected. CEO John Stankey said the telecom is accelerating plans to send roughly $US10 billion to shareholders this year through share buybacks.

Advertisement

Super Micro Computer soared 20 per cent after the seller of AI servers said it expects to report stronger profit margins for the latest quarter than it had earlier forecast. It, though, also said that revenue will likely come in at the low end of its forecasted range . They helped offset an 8.7 per cent drop for GE Vernova, which reported a weaker profit for the latest quarter than analysts expected. Stocks broadly also felt pressure from continuing climbs for oil prices, which raise costs for most businesses and erode their profits. Related Article Opinion

Media & marketing It’s News Corp v the ‘byte burglars’: Murdochs fire up in AI content battle Elizabeth Knight Business columnist The price for a barrel of Brent crude oil, the international standard, rose 3.3 per cent to $US94 after briefly topping $US95 in the morning to touch its highest price in nearly six weeks. That’s up from less than $US72 early this month, which is roughly where it was before the war.

Advertisement

Rising oil prices are threatening a reacceleration of inflation. That in turn could push the Federal Reserve and other central banks to raise interest rates, which would slow economies and undercut prices for stocks and other investments. The yield on the 10-year Treasury edged up to 4.66 per cent from 4.63 per cent late Tuesday and from just 3.97 per cent before the war with Iran began. It’s already helped bring long-term US mortgage rates to their highest levels in nearly a year. Oil prices have climbed as fighting across the Middle East keeps oil tankers from using the Strait of Hormuz to exit the Persian Gulf. Normally, a fifth of all oil and natural gas traded passes through the narrow strait. In other international markets, indexes climbed in Europe following a mixed session in Asia. London’s FTSE 100 rose 1.2 per cent, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng fell 1 per cent for two of the world’s bigger moves. with AP, Reuters The Market Recap newsletter is a wrap of the day’s trading. title: Money to burn? Big Tech’s AI splurge is blowing out
text:
Advertisement

BusinessCompaniesAI Opinion Money to burn? Big Tech’s AI splurge is blowing out Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist July 23, 2026 — 12:01pm

July 23, 2026 — 12:01pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Amid a raft of impressive numbers in Alphabet’s second-quarter results, one stands out. While quadrupling its profits, Google’s parent company reported the first quarter of negative free cash flow in its history. The apparent massive lift in profit overstated Alphabet’s operating performance by including a $US98 billion ($140 billion) non-cash mark-up in the value of its investments in SpaceX and Anthropic, but the cash-burn in the quarter was very real. The cash burn is real: Alphabet has spent up big to make Google an AI giant. Getty It has become a common feature of big technology companies’ results as they plough ever-increasing amounts of cash and capital into artificial intelligence. In fact, both of those features of Alphabet’s numbers are common to the “hyperscalers” chasing their places in an AI-dominated future and share a common thread: the demands for capital to fund AI developments outstrip the ability of the companies to fund them internally.

Advertisement

Increasingly, even the biggest tech companies are calling on debt markets, raising equity from the sharemarket and investing in their rivals to create daisy chains of mutual dependence – and vulnerability. The demands aren’t diminishing. Alphabet had capital expenditures of $US44.9 billion in the quarter – more than double the amount invested the same quarter of last year, and about 25 per cent more than it spent in the first quarter. The falls in Alphabet and Tesla’s share prices after their results suggest that investors’ patience and tolerance for the ever-escalating costs of their AI plans are starting to wane. It had previously forecast capex of $US180 billion to $US190 billion for the full year, but has now raised that to $US195 billion to $US205 billion. Alphabet wasn’t the only big tech company reporting results where AI investing overshadowed the performance of its more established operations.

Advertisement

Elon Musk’s Tesla also released its June-quarter results this morning, which showed profits stayed essentially flat at $US1.1 billion, despite a 25 per cent jump in revenue. Tesla generates more than 70 per cent of its revenue from sales of its electric cars – it sold a lot more of them in the quarter, albeit with significantly lower margins – but it is trying to transition to an AI-facilitated future of Robotaxis and robots. Related Article AI OpenAI says its AI models went rogue and attacked a digital library It, too, experienced negative cash flows in the quarter as it ploughed $US5.8 billion into spending, much of it AI- and robotics-related. That was more that twice its $US2.5 billion of capex in the first quarter, but well short of the rate needed to meet the $US25 billion of investment Musk now says he is targeting for the full-year. For both Alphabet and Tesla there are clear signs of surging AI-related sales growth.

Advertisement

Alphabet’s cloud business revenues surged 82 per cent and its increasingly AI-integrated search unit also seems to be benefiting, while subscriptions to Tesla’s “Full-Self-Driving” automated driver-assist software, which costs $US99 a month, grew 15.6 per cent to 1.5 million. That’s a 56 per cent increase on their level a year ago. Both companies’ problem, and that of the entire AI sector, is that revenue growth rates are being outstripped by the growth rates in the spending required to keep up with the development of AI. Not only is the competition in AI increasingly being driven by the hardware – semiconductors, servers, data centres – but the intense competition, and the demand it is generating, is straining the capacity of the industry’s “picks and shovels” companies to deliver. The costs of “compute” are rising sharply and inexorably, and the timelines between committing the capital and generating revenue from it are starting to blow out. The falls in both companies’ share prices after their results (Alphabet was down 1.5 per cent and Tesla dropped 1.3 per cent) suggest that investors’ patience and tolerance for the ever-escalating costs of their AI plans are starting to wane.

Advertisement

Related Article AI Google is building an AI fence around the internet it once championed It’s not helping the companies or the environment in which they are seeking to raise capital that China seems to have a proliferation of open-source AI companies that can deliver near-comparable performance at a fraction of the cost of the hyperscalers, although the established tech giants do have the advantage of being able to integrate AI into their existing businesses and customer bases. Alphabet, like the other US hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, Oracle), used to be a “capital-light” business. Today, it and its peers are increasingly capital-intensive as the impact of the $US750 billion-plus they plan to spend this year – and the $US1 trillion-plus next year – continues to hit their balance sheets. Their financial profiles are being transformed, and their balance sheets are becoming leveraged. For instance, Alphabet’s total asset base has grown from $US595.3 billion a year ago to $US922 billion, with its long-term debt more than doubling from $US46.6 billion to $US98.2 billion to help fund that expansion. It also raised $US50 billion selling new shares as part of a planned $US80 billion capital raising.

Advertisement

At least the hyperscalers, and Tesla for that matter, have cashflows and earnings from their pre-existing businesses to help fund their AI investments. Start-ups like OpenAI and Anthropic don’t have those legacy cashflows and therefore are reliant on regular equity injections from investors (including their competitors) and, increasingly, bond issues. The sharemarket performance of SpaceX sounds a warning note, suggesting that not all things AI always rise in value. Getty Images There’s a circularity to the relationships between the industry participants that has so far enabled valuations to keep rising, which provides the necessary access to equity and debt markets (and paper profits from shareholdings in AI-driven companies). The sharemarket performance of another one of Musk’s companies, SpaceX, however, sounds a warning note suggesting that not all things AI always rise in value.

Advertisement

SpaceX debuted on the sharemarket last month at an initial public offering price of $US135 a share. The stock opened above $US150, spiked above $US200 within its first fortnight of trading – and is currently priced at about $US115. Related Article Opinion

Sharemarket Is SpaceX a meme stock? Elon Musk loses trillionaire status Kishor Napier-Raman Senior Business Writer The company has lost more than $US1 trillion of its peak market valuation and, with nearly another billion of “insider” shares coming out of lockup early next month, is at risk of falling further. SpaceX’s early market performance was an illustration of investors’ willingness to capitalise visions of what AI might be. Its subsequent fall signals that they also want some evidence that the vast amounts being invested in AI today will produce a return, commensurate with the risks, at some point in the not-too-distant future. title: Waterhouse-Bott win race for Leilani Lodge to spark major stable move
text:
The Australian Turf Club has cited safety concerns as a major factor in granting Leilani Lodge to Gai Waterhouse and Adrian Bott in a move that is set to signal the end of Tulloch Lodge, Sydney’s most successful stables.

The ATC announced on Thursday that Waterhouse and Bott would take over the lease of 65 boxes at the High Street stables established and made famous by Melbourne Cup king Bart Cummings from the early 1980s.

Trainer Gai Waterhouse with Just Fine at her Randwick stables in 2023 Kate Geraghty

Leilani Lodge was vacated early last year after Cummings’ son, Anthony, was stripped of his trainers’ licence and evicted. Ciaron Maher then beat Waterhouse and Bott to secure the lease, but the top Victorian trainer, a three-time Australian premiership winner, had not moved any horses in after months of significant refurbishments then a change of his plans to instead take over at Racing NSW-bought 125-box Crown Lodge at Warwick Farm.

Waterhouse and Bott this time edged out Michael Freedman on the short-list for Leilani Lodge, although he will be given an additional 20 boxes on course in a rejig of other stables.

title: Billion-dollar charity locked in $923m tax fight with the ATO
text:
Advertisement

Exclusive NationalPhilanthropy Billion-dollar charity locked in $923m tax fight with the ATO Patrick Begley July 23, 2026 — 11:47am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

His gardener and receptionist became millionaires overnight. His best friend and “right hand” man received $400 million. But the greatest beneficiary of Paul Ramsay’s will was his charity. Paul Ramsay died in 2014, leaving a fortune of more than $3.5 billion. Now, more than a decade after the private healthcare pioneer’s sudden death, the Paul Ramsay Foundation is locked in a $923 million fight with the Australian Taxation Office over income tax. Headquartered in a sandstone building with elaborate forged-steel gates in Sydney’s Darlinghurst, the foundation funds university research, Indigenous ventures and anti-poverty initiatives.

Advertisement

It also underpins the Ramsay Centre for Western Civilisation, a separate and at times contentious humanities program that champions a Western canon and values. The centre, whose board includes former prime minister Tony Abbott, received $37 million from the foundation in 2024-2025. The Darlinghurst headquarters of the Paul Ramsay Foundation. Louise Kennerley The tax fight, according to financial accounts submitted to the charities watchdog, stems from “legacy matters arising from the administration of the estate of the late Paul Ramsay”. Single, and with no children, Ramsay was worth about $3.5 billion when he succumbed to a suspected heart attack while sailing in Spain, aged 78. His fortune came from the private hospital empire he had built over decades, starting with an old guesthouse on Sydney’s north shore, which he converted into a psychiatric facility in 1964.

Advertisement

Ramsay Healthcare today trades publicly at a valuation of nearly $10 billion, operating across Australia, the United Kingdom and Europe. Related Article Education The $3.5 billion will, the instant millionaires ... and what was left out After the founder’s death, ownership of shares he owned in the company was transferred to a private ancillary fund – a vehicle often used by the wealthy to bankroll charitable ventures – as part of a $3 billion bequest to the foundation. The Ramsay fund is at the centre of the dispute with the tax office, which conducted an income tax audit for the years 2016, 2017 and 2018. “The total primary tax amount levied on the [fund] is $699 million,” the charity wrote in its annual accounts, which also referred to a $175 million penalty and $49 million in interest charges.

Advertisement

The fund objects to the income tax assessments. If unsuccessful, the Paul Ramsay Foundation will foot the bill. A foundation spokesman said the tax audit focused on steps taken in 2016 to administer Ramsay’s estate after he “died unexpectedly”. Related Article Investigation

Philanthropy ‘Next shiny thing’: Inside Twiggy Forrest’s $7 billion charity “The steps were taken with the benefit of tax advice from a leading accounting firm, as well as independent legal advice,” the spokesman said. He said the dispute did not relate to the foundation’s “charitable purpose or operations” and “does not impact the charity’s funding partners”. Those partners include the NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service, the University of Newcastle, RMIT, The Royal Women’s Hospital in Melbourne, the Queensland Performing Arts Trust, The Australia Institute and The Smith Family.

Advertisement

The foundation says it has distributed “more than $1.5 billion in funding to charities seeking to address disadvantage in Australia” since 2017. But the value of its assets has declined with falls in Ramsay Healthcare’s share price. A foundation subsidiary has sold shares in recent years to meet “cashflow needs”. A tax office spokesperson declined to comment, citing “obligations under taxpayer confidentiality laws”.
